rate_basis: Statutory "legal rate" of interest under Ala. Code § 8-8-1: $6 upon $100 for one year (6% per annum). This is the default prejudgment rate a court applies. For contract claims, interest runs at the rate stated in the contract; a written-contract rate may go up to 8% (§ 8-8-1 cap), otherwise the 6% legal rate applies.
statute_cite: Ala. Code § 8-8-1 (legal rate of interest, 6%); Ala. Code § 8-8-8 (interest accrues on breach of contract); Nelson v. AmSouth Bank, N.A., 622 So. 2d 894 (Ala. 1993) (prejudgment interest at the legal rate in noncontract cases only where damages are ascertainable by mere computation).
formula: Fixed 6% per annum (the § 8-8-1 legal rate). Not indexed/variable. For contract claims, use the contract's stated rate instead (written-contract rate capped at 8% under § 8-8-1); absent a stated rate, the 6% legal rate applies.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is available ONLY on liquidated / reasonably ascertainable sums. (1) Contract claims (§ 8-8-8): recoverable as of right where the amount is certain or ascertainable at the time of breach; a bona fide dispute over the amount does not defeat it if the sum is computable by known standards. (2) Noncontract/tort claims: recoverable at the 6% legal rate ONLY where damages "can be ascertained by mere computation" or are complete/certain at a given time by known standards of value (Nelson v. AmSouth Bank). (3) BARRED on unliquidated damages that are not ascertainable until the factfinder fixes them — this excludes typical personal-injury and most general tort damages (pain/suffering, etc.). No special PI-specific prejudgment-interest statute; PI unliquidated damages generally get none. Punitive damages do not bear prejudgment interest.
accrual: Contract claims: from the day the money should have been paid or the act performed (§ 8-8-8 — "from the day such money... should have been paid"), i.e., the date of breach. Noncontract/tort claims (where allowed): from the date of injury/loss, provided the property injured or destroyed has an ascertainable money value.
simple_or_compound: Simple
confidence_notes: High confidence. Statutory text of Ala. Code § 8-8-1 ($6 upon $100/year = 6%) and § 8-8-8 confirmed verbatim via primary-text mirror (onecle); official Alabama Legislature Code site (alison.legislature.state.al.us) hosts the same sections but is JS-rendered so text couldn't be extracted via fetch. The liquidated/ascertainable-only restriction and accrual rules are well-settled Alabama case doctrine (Nelson v. AmSouth Bank, 622 So. 2d 894). Note distinction from POST-judgment interest: money judgments are 7.5% (contract judgments at the contract rate) under § 8-8-10 for judgments entered on/after Sept. 1, 2011 — that is a separate, higher figure and should not be confused with the 6% prejudgment legal rate reported here.

rate_basis: For judgments entered in 2026, the rate is 6.75% per year, as published by the Alaska Court System on official form ADM-505 (rev. 1/26). The rate is set annually as three percentage points above the 12th Federal Reserve District discount rate in effect on January 2 of the year the judgment/decree is entered. The same rate applies to both prejudgment and post-judgment interest. The rate is fixed as of the judgment year and does not change thereafter for that judgment.
statute_cite: AS 09.30.070 (rate formula in subsection (a); accrual in subsection (b); exclusions in subsection (c))
formula: Three percentage points above the 12th Federal Reserve District discount rate in effect on January 2 of the year in which the judgment or decree is entered (AS 09.30.070(a)). Discount rate published by the Federal Reserve at https://www.frbdiscountwindow.org/pages/discount-rates. Same formula governs both prejudgment and post-judgment interest. EXCEPTION: a judgment founded on a written contract that specifies an interest rate (not exceeding the legal rate for that contract) bears interest at the contract rate if set out in the judgment.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is a general remedy in Alaska and is NOT limited to liquidated or contract claims — it is recoverable on tort and unliquidated damages as compensation for loss of use of money. However, by statute AS 09.30.070(c), prejudgment interest may NOT be awarded on: (1) future economic damages, (2) future noneconomic damages, or (3) punitive damages. Where a written contract specifies an interest rate, that contract rate controls instead of the statutory rate. No special personal-injury-only carve-out beyond the future-damages exclusion (which is significant in PI cases, since future medical/wage losses get no prejudgment interest).
accrual: Accrues from the earlier of (a) the day process is served on the defendant, or (b) the day the defendant received written notification that an injury occurred and that a claim may be brought — the notice being of a nature that would lead a prudent person to believe a claim will be made — unless the parties have agreed otherwise (AS 09.30.070(b)). (Note: for older cases, the accrual/rate rules differ; complaints filed July 1, 1980–Aug 7, 1997 used a flat 10.5%.)
simple_or_compound: simple
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence. Current rate (6.75% for 2026) comes directly from the Alaska Court System's official published form ADM-505 (rev. 1/26), which states verbatim: "For judgments entered in 2026, this rate is 6.75%." The form and statute (AS 09.30.070) confirm the same rate applies to both pre- and post-judgment interest and cite the exact statutory formula. Statutory restrictions (no prejudgment interest on future economic damages, future noneconomic damages, or punitive damages) confirmed via AS 09.30.070(c) text. Accrual language confirmed via AS 09.30.070(b). Rate is variable (reset each judgment year via the Fed discount rate) but published as a determinable annual number by the courts. Simple (not compound) interest is settled Alaska law. The akleg.gov statute page returned 403 to automated fetch, but the Alaska Courts form and Justia/FindLaw statute reproductions are consistent. Prior-year rates from ADM-505: 2025=7.5%, 2024=8.5%, 2023=7.5%, 2021-2022=3.25%, 2020=5.25%, 2019=6%.

rate_basis: Lesser of 10% per year OR (prime rate + 1%), per A.R.S. 44-1201(B). Prime rate is 6.75% as of July 8, 2026 (Federal Reserve H.15), so prime + 1% = 7.75%, which is less than 10% — thus 7.75% is the current default prejudgment rate. Exception: where the obligation is based on a written contract specifying a different rate, subsection (A)'s contracted rate applies instead of the 10%/prime+1% cap.
statute_cite: A.R.S. § 44-1201(A), (B) & (F); see Metzler v. BCI Coca-Cola Bottling Co., 235 Ariz. 141 (App. 2014) (subsection B lesser-of formula applies absent a written contract rate)
formula: Variable: lesser of 10%/year or (1% + prime rate published in Federal Reserve statistical release H.15). Prime is currently 6.75%, so the applicable rate is 6.75% + 1% = 7.75%. A rate change takes effect on the first business day following publication by the Federal Reserve; but once a judgment is entered it states the applicable rate and that rate does not change thereafter (A.R.S. 44-1201(F)). If a written contract sets a different rate, that contracted rate governs (subsection A).
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is available ONLY on LIQUIDATED / readily-ascertainable claims — a claim is liquidated if the plaintiff provides a basis for precisely calculating the amount owed. A.R.S. 44-1201(F) EXPRESSLY BARS a court from awarding prejudgment interest on (1) unliquidated damages, (2) future damages, (3) punitive damages, and (4) exemplary damages found by the trier of fact. Prejudgment interest is NOT limited to contract claims — it is available on any liquidated claim including tort claims where damages are certain/ascertainable (e.g., liquidated tort claims), but unliquidated tort/personal-injury (pain-and-suffering) damages do not qualify. No PI-specific statutory formula; PI awards for non-economic loss are unliquidated and thus barred.
accrual: Prejudgment interest accrues from the date the claim becomes liquidated / due and demandable — i.e., the date the amount owed became certain or capable of precise calculation (for a liquidated debt, typically the date of the demand or the date payment was due). It runs until entry of judgment. (Accrual date is set by Arizona case law, not by the text of 44-1201.)
simple_or_compound: Simple — Arizona applies simple (non-compounding) interest to judgments/prejudgment interest absent a written agreement providing otherwise.
confidence_notes: High confidence. Rate mechanics verified against the official Arizona Legislature statute text (azleg.gov) for A.R.S. 44-1201: subsection A = 10%/yr default (or contracted written rate); subsection B = lesser of 10% or prime+1% for judgments other than medical debt; the prejudgment sentence ("If awarded, prejudgment interest shall be at the rate described in subsection A or B"); and the 44-1201(F) bar on prejudgment interest for unliquidated/future/punitive/exemplary damages. Current prime rate 6.75% confirmed from Federal Reserve H.15 (July 8, 2026), yielding 7.75% today. Note: statute is variable-capped — 7.75% is today's number and moves with the prime rate until judgment entry (44-1201(F) freezes it at entry). The liquidated-claim requirement and accrual-date rule are Arizona common-law doctrine (e.g., Gemstar, Homes & Son Construction, Metzler) rather than statutory text; law-firm summaries used only to locate the primary sources. Note: a separate special medical-debt cap (lesser of 1-yr CMT yield or 3%) applies to medical-debt judgments and is not the general default.

rate_basis: Federal Reserve primary credit rate (3.75% as of the H.15 release dated 2026-07-09, effective 2026-07-08) in effect on the date judgment is entered, PLUS two percent (2%) = 5.75%. For a contract action, the rate is the greater of the contract's stated rate or this Fed+2% figure. Capped by Arkansas Constitution Amendment 89 (max 17% on general loans/contracts). Note: this is the same rate formula the statute assigns to post-judgment interest; the rate is fixed as of the judgment date and does not float thereafter.
statute_cite: Ark. Code Ann. § 16-65-114(a)(1) (as amended by Act 995 of 2019); rate cap under Ark. Const. amend. 89, § 3. Common-law availability standard: Woodline Motor Freight, Inc. v. Troutman Oil Co., 327 Ark. 448, 938 S.W.2d 565 (1997).
formula: Contract actions: greater of (a) contract rate or (b) Federal Reserve primary credit rate on the judgment date + 2%. All other (non-contract/tort) actions: Federal Reserve primary credit rate on the judgment date + 2%. Fed primary credit rate is published in the Federal Reserve H.15 Selected Interest Rates release (currently 3.75%), so the current default prejudgment rate = 5.75%. Rate may not exceed the Amendment 89 maximum (17% for general contracts).
restrictions: CRITICAL NUANCE — availability is narrow and governed by common law, not the statute (the 2019 statute supplies only the RATE). Prejudgment interest is allowed ONLY when the amount of damages is definitely/reasonably ascertainable at the time of loss — i.e., fixed by a mathematical computation or by rules of evidence/known standards WITHOUT reliance on opinion or the fact-finder's discretion (Woodline Motor Freight v. Troutman Oil, 327 Ark. 448 (1997)). So: liquidated/ascertainable contract debts, conversion of property with a market value, and similar claims DO get it. Unliquidated claims do NOT — this effectively BARS prejudgment interest on personal-injury torts (pain and suffering, future medicals, lost earning capacity are speculative), on general unliquidated tort damages, and on punitive damages. When prejudgment interest IS available it is a matter of right (mandatory), not discretionary. The statute's phrase 'if appropriate under the facts of the case' is the hook that incorporates this ascertainability test.
accrual: Accrues from the date of the loss/injury (the date the ascertainable amount was owed or the property was taken/damaged), running up to the date of judgment. It does not accrue until/unless damages became definitely ascertainable.
simple_or_compound: Simple
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence on statutory rate; MODERATE-HIGH on framing. Statute text confirmed from FindLaw (official-mirror) and Justia: Ark. Code § 16-65-114(a)(1) was rewritten by Act 995 of 2019 to expressly add prejudgment interest and tie it to the Fed primary credit rate + 2% (previously the field applied only the constitutional/common-law legal rate of 6% under Ark. Const. art. 19, § 13). The Fed primary credit rate of 3.75% is confirmed from the official Federal Reserve H.15 release dated 2026-07-09 (rate effective 2026-07-08), giving a current default of 5.75%. IMPORTANT CAVEAT flagged by practitioners (e.g., Mitchell Williams law firm, 'What is Arkansas Pre-Judgment Interest Rate? A Vexing Question'): some Arkansas cases decided after 2019 have continued to reference the old 6% common-law/constitutional rate, so there is genuine uncertainty in practice about whether courts apply the new § 16-65-114 formula (Fed+2%) or the legacy 6% legal rate to PREjudgment interest specifically — the statute is arguably framed around 'judgment' interest and courts have not uniformly extended the new rate backward. Reported figure uses the current statutory formula (5.75%). If a court instead applies the constitutional legal rate, the figure would be 6.00%. Availability restrictions (ascertainability, tort/PI exclusion) are well-settled and unaffected by the rate question. Because the rate is variable (keyed to Fed primary credit rate on judgment date), the 5.75% figure is only the value for a judgment entered on today's Fed rate.

rate_basis: Rate depends on claim type (not a single universal figure). CONTRACT (no rate stipulated, contract entered after 1/1/1986): 10% per annum, mandatory after breach (Civ. Code sec. 3289(b)). LIQUIDATED NON-CONTRACT claims (damages certain or ascertainable): 7% per annum, the constitutional default legal rate (Cal. Const. art. XV sec. 1) applied via Civ. Code sec. 3287(a). DISCRETIONARY TORT interest (Civ. Code sec. 3288): 7%. Any interest rate actually stipulated in the contract governs instead (Civ. Code sec. 3289(a)).
statute_cite: Cal. Civ. Code sec. 3287 (liquidated/ascertainable, mandatory sec. 3287(a); unliquidated contract, discretionary sec. 3287(b)); Cal. Civ. Code sec. 3288 (discretionary tort/fraud/malice); Cal. Civ. Code sec. 3289 (contract: stipulated rate survives breach, or 10% default for post-1/1/1986 contracts with no stipulated rate); Cal. Const. art. XV sec. 1 (7% default legal rate)
formula: Fixed statutory/constitutional percentages, not a published index. Contract w/o stipulated rate (post-1/1/1986): 10%/yr simple (Civ. Code 3289(b)). Other liquidated claims / discretionary tort: 7%/yr simple (Cal. Const. art. XV sec. 1). Contract with a stipulated legal rate: that rate continues after breach (Civ. Code 3289(a)).
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is NOT automatic on all claims. MANDATORY (as of right) only where damages are "certain, or capable of being made certain by calculation" and the right vested on a particular day — i.e., liquidated/readily ascertainable claims (Civ. Code sec. 3287(a)). UNLIQUIDATED tort claims: interest is BARRED as of right; it is DISCRETIONARY with the jury, and only in actions for breach of a non-contract obligation or cases of oppression, fraud, or malice (Civ. Code sec. 3288). UNLIQUIDATED contract claims: discretionary with the court, but no earlier than the date the action was filed (Civ. Code sec. 3287(b)). Personal-injury / non-economic (pain and suffering) damages are generally NOT recoverable with prejudgment interest because they are not ascertainable until verdict; PI plaintiffs can, however, recover prejudgment interest on certain liquidated economic damages, and CCP sec. 998 offers can trigger interest. Punitive damages do NOT bear prejudgment interest. Future damages do not accrue prejudgment interest for the period before they are incurred.
accrual: For mandatory liquidated claims (sec. 3287(a)): from the day the right to recover vested / damages became certain (e.g., date of loss or breach). For contract default 10% (sec. 3289(b)): from the date of breach. For discretionary unliquidated contract interest (sec. 3287(b)): from a date fixed by the court, but no earlier than the date the action was filed. For discretionary tort interest (sec. 3288): date is within the jury's discretion.
simple_or_compound: Simple. California prejudgment interest is computed as simple interest; compounding is not authorized absent a contract term providing for it.
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence — all figures verified against primary/official sources on leginfo.legislature.ca.gov (Cal. Civ. Code sec. 3287, 3288, 3289 and Cal. Const. art. XV sec. 1). Key nuance: California has no single prejudgment rate. rate_kind marked "variable" because the applicable rate turns on claim type (10% contract default vs. 7% constitutional default) rather than a time-varying index; it is NOT tied to Treasury/Fed rates. Reported current_rate_percent=10 reflects the most common prejudgment scenario (breach of contract with no stipulated rate under sec. 3289(b)); the 7% constitutional rate applies to other liquidated non-contract claims and discretionary tort awards. Discretionary categories (sec. 3287(b), sec. 3288) still have a determinable rate a court applies (7%), so verified=true. Post-judgment rate is separately 10% under CCP sec. 685.010 — not the same statutory basis as prejudgment.

rate_basis: Fixed statutory rate set by the legislature in C.R.S. 5-12-102: 8% per annum for the general prejudgment/moneys-withheld category. This is a hard-coded statutory number (not indexed/variable). A separate fixed rate of 9% per annum applies to personal-injury/tort bodily-injury damages under C.R.S. 13-21-101. Note: a distinct variable, market-determined rate under 5-12-106 / 13-21-101(3)-(4) applies to money judgments that are APPEALED — that is post-judgment/appeal interest, not the default prejudgment rate.
statute_cite: C.R.S. 5-12-102 (general statutory interest, 8%); C.R.S. 13-21-101 (personal-injury damages, 9%)
formula: Fixed value. General: 8% per annum, compounded annually (C.R.S. 5-12-102(1)(b)). Personal injury: 9% per annum, compounded annually for actions filed on/after 7/1/1979 (C.R.S. 13-21-101(1)). NOT indexed — no formula/publisher. (Appealed-judgment interest is a separate variable rate = 1 year T-bill + points, published by the CO Secretary of State, but that is not the default prejudgment rate.)
restrictions: CRITICAL nuance — Colorado is UNUSUALLY BROAD: prejudgment interest is NOT limited to liquidated/ascertainable sums. Under 5-12-102, the claimant is entitled to interest on all moneys or property "wrongfully withheld," and Colorado courts (e.g., Mesa Sand & Gravel v. Landfill) have held this covers UNLIQUIDATED claims and general contract/property damages — the amount need not be certain or ascertainable. So it is available on unliquidated claims, unlike most states. Personal injury / tort bodily-injury claims are carved out into 13-21-101 at 9% (interest treated as an element of compensatory damages, accruing from the date the action accrued). Medical debt is capped at 3% per annum (5-12-102(1); definition via 5-16-103). Contractually agreed rates control if the parties agreed to one (5-12-102(1) applies only "when there is no agreement as to the rate"). Prejudgment interest is generally NOT awarded on future damages and not on punitive/exemplary damages. Claimant may ELECT under 5-12-102(1)(a) the actual gain/benefit realized by the withholder instead of the flat 8%.
accrual: General (5-12-102): from the date the money/property was wrongfully withheld or the date it became due, running to date of payment or date judgment is entered, whichever occurs first. Personal injury (13-21-101): plaintiff may claim interest from the date the action accrued (for actions filed on/after 7/1/1979), or from the date suit was filed, running to the date of satisfying the judgment.
simple_or_compound: Compound — compounded annually under both 5-12-102 (8%) and 13-21-101 (9%, compounded annually for actions filed on/after 7/1/1979).
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence. Both rates verified verbatim from official/primary source colorado.public.law (mirrors the enacted Colorado Revised Statutes): 5-12-102(1)(b) = "eight percent per annum compounded annually" for moneys/property wrongfully withheld or due; 13-21-101(1) = "nine percent per annum" for personal-injury actions filed on/after 7/1/1975, compounded annually for actions filed on/after 7/1/1979. Cross-confirmed via Justia and FindLaw statutory mirrors. The 8% figure is the default general prejudgment rate a court applies today and has been stable (unchanged since the statute's current form). Two caveats reported in rate_basis/restrictions: (1) 9% PI carve-out under 13-21-101; (2) a SEPARATE variable rate applies to APPEALED money judgments (post-judgment/appeal interest, published annually by the CO Secretary of State at coloradosos.gov) — that is not the prejudgment default and should not be confused with it. Medical-debt sub-cap of 3% noted.

rate_basis: Statutory rate/ceiling fixed by Conn. Gen. Stat. § 37-3a(a): "interest at the rate of ten per cent a year, and no more." The 10% is both the maximum and the rate courts routinely apply when they exercise discretion to award prejudgment interest. Special sub-rule: debt arising out of hospital services is capped at 5% per year (also discretionary).
statute_cite: Conn. Gen. Stat. § 37-3a(a) (general prejudgment interest, 10%). Related: § 37-3b (negligence/injury actions — post-judgment only, 10%); § 52-192a (offer-of-compromise interest); § 37-3c (hospital debt). Chapter 673, Title 37.
formula: Fixed at 10% per year (ceiling). Not indexed to any published benchmark. Hospital-services debt: capped at 5% per year. Both are discretionary/equitable, not automatic.
restrictions: CRITICAL: § 37-3a prejudgment interest is available ONLY as "damages for the detention of money after it becomes payable" — i.e., a LIQUIDATED or readily ascertainable sum that was wrongfully withheld after it became due (breach of contract, unpaid debts, wrongfully retained deposits/payments, ascertainable amounts). Two-part test: (1) claim is for a liquidated sum wrongfully withheld, and (2) equitable considerations warrant interest. BARRED on unliquidated damages — the CT courts hold prejudgment interest under 37-3a is NOT available for the uncertain/unliquidated damages typical of personal-injury negligence, defamation, or tortious-interference claims (damages not "already payable"). PERSONAL INJURY / NEGLIGENCE: governed instead by § 37-3b, which grants interest at 10% but only POST-judgment (runs from the earlier of 20 days after judgment or 90 days after verdict) — so no prejudgment interest for negligence/injury-to-person-or-property torts. Punitive/future damages: not "money detained after payable," so outside 37-3a. Award is always in the trial court's equitable discretion (fair-and-equitable standard), and 37-3a interest stops accruing at the date of judgment ("and no more").
accrual: Interest runs from the date the money became due and payable / the date it was wrongfully withheld (i.e., the date the court determines the money was due), through the date of judgment. Not from date of filing.
simple_or_compound: Simple. The statute does not specify compounding; Connecticut Supreme Court/appellate case law (e.g., Sears, Roebuck & Co. v. Board of Tax Review; Foley v. Huntington Co.) treats § 37-3a interest as simple interest absent a contractual provision for compounding.
confidence_notes: High confidence. Statutory text confirmed from the official State of Connecticut government portal (portal.ct.gov, CGS as amended to Jan 1, 2021) and corroborated across the CT General Assembly chapter listing (cga.ct.gov, whose direct fetch failed only on a TLS certificate error, not content). Rate is unambiguously 10% fixed under § 37-3a(a) and has been stable for decades. The "discretionary-with-default" classification reflects that CT prejudgment interest is an equitable award at the court's discretion, but with a fixed default/ceiling rate of 10% that courts apply — so a determinable rate exists (verified=true). Key nuance well-settled in case law: 37-3a requires a liquidated/ascertainable sum wrongfully detained; unliquidated tort/PI damages are excluded and negligence-injury actions instead get post-judgment-only interest under § 37-3b. Law-firm articles were used only to locate/confirm official text, not as primary authority.

rate_basis: Federal Reserve discount rate (primary credit rate) plus 5%. As of July 2026 the Fed primary credit/discount rate is 3.75%, so the legal rate is 3.75% + 5% = 8.75%. The rate is fixed for a given claim at the value of (Fed discount rate + 5%) as of the date interest begins to accrue (date of breach / when payment became due), and does not float thereafter; but it varies across cases with the Fed discount rate, so it is a formula-based/variable rate rather than a single statutory number.
statute_cite: 6 Del. C. § 2301(a) (legal rate); prejudgment interest awarded under this legal rate by Delaware common law (contract) and 6 Del. C. § 2301(d) (tort compensatory damages)
formula: Legal rate = Federal Reserve discount rate (including any surcharge) + 5%, measured "as of the time from which interest is due" (6 Del. C. § 2301(a)). Currently 3.75% Fed discount rate + 5% = 8.75%. Rate is locked at the date interest starts accruing and applied as simple interest by default.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest under Delaware law is awarded as a MATTER OF RIGHT (not discretionary) to a prevailing plaintiff on a liquidated or ascertainable money claim, most clearly in breach-of-contract actions where a sum is due. It compensates for the loss of use of money and is available even where damages were unliquidated/disputed, as long as a definite sum is ultimately owed; a contract (or contract-type money) claim generally must be pleaded for prejudgment interest to accrue (Superior Court has held a plaintiff who did not plead a contract claim could not recover prejudgment interest). For TORT actions seeking compensatory damages for bodily injury, death, or property damage (including personal injury), § 2301(d) governs and adds a special precondition: interest runs from the date of injury ONLY if the plaintiff made a written settlement demand, valid for at least 30 days, in an amount LESS than the damages ultimately awarded — otherwise the § 2301(d) date-of-injury prejudgment interest does not attach. Punitive damages do not bear prejudgment interest. Purely future damages are not a base for prejudgment interest. The Court of Chancery applies prejudgment interest at the § 2301 legal rate but retains equitable discretion to adjust the amount (e.g., reduce it for a plaintiff's undue delay) and, in appraisal (8 Del. C. § 262) and some fiduciary-duty matters, may compound rather than use simple interest.
accrual: Contract claims: from the date of breach / the date payment became due (when the money should have been paid). Tort compensatory damages under § 2301(d): from the date of injury, but only if the qualifying written settlement demand (valid 30+ days, below the final award) was made. Rate is fixed at the Fed-discount-rate-plus-5% value in effect on that accrual date.
simple_or_compound: Simple by default. Delaware disfavors compound interest and courts award simple prejudgment interest absent an express contractual or statutory provision permitting compounding; compounding appears mainly in statutory appraisal actions (8 Del. C. § 262) and certain breach-of-fiduciary-duty cases within Chancery's equitable discretion.
confidence_notes: High confidence. Primary source: official Delaware Code (delcode.delaware.gov, 6 Del. C. § 2301) confirms the legal rate is "5% over the Federal Reserve discount rate including any surcharge as of the time from which interest is due," and § 2301(d) confirms the tort date-of-injury rule conditioned on a 30-day written settlement demand below the award. Current Fed primary credit/discount rate of 3.75% (Federal Reserve H.15, July 2026) yields a current legal/prejudgment rate of 8.75%. Delaware case law (Court of Chancery / Superior Court, via Morris James and Delaware Trial Handbook summaries used only to locate the holdings) confirms: prejudgment interest is a matter of right on money/contract claims; accrual from date of breach; simple interest default; rate fixed as of accrual date. One nuance: the number is not a single fixed statutory figure — it is a formula tied to the Fed discount rate, so "8.75%" is the value as of the as_of_date and changes with the Fed discount rate for claims accruing at other times.

rate_basis: Statutory rate set quarterly by the Florida Chief Financial Officer under Fla. Stat. § 55.03(1): the average of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York discount rate for the preceding 12 months, plus 400 basis points. The same statutory rate that applies to judgments applies to prejudgment interest. Current published rate is 8.06% per annum (0.000220822/day) effective for the quarter beginning July 1, 2026.
statute_cite: Fla. Stat. § 55.03 (rate-setting mechanism); § 687.01 (default legal rate where no contract rate specified); prejudgment-interest entitlement/method governed by common law — Argonaut Ins. Co. v. May Plumbing Co., 474 So. 2d 212 (Fla. 1985) ("loss theory")
formula: Prejudgment interest accrues at the § 55.03 statutory rate in effect during each period from the date of loss to the date of judgment. § 55.03 rate = 12-month average of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York discount rate + 400 basis points, reset quarterly (Dec 1 / Mar 1 / Jun 1 / Sep 1) by the CFO. Because the rate changes quarterly over a multi-quarter accrual window, prejudgment interest is computed period-by-period at each quarter's published rate (the rate is NOT fixed as of the loss date, unlike the post-judgment rate which fixes at judgment and adjusts each Jan 1). Recent quarterly rates: 8.06% (Jul 1, 2026), 8.25% (Apr 1, 2026), 8.44% (Jan 1, 2026), 8.65% (Oct 1, 2025), 8.90% (Jul 1, 2025), 9.15% (Apr 1, 2025), 9.38% (Jan 1, 2025).
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is available ONLY on LIQUIDATED / readily ascertainable damages representing an actual out-of-pocket pecuniary loss fixed as of a date certain (Argonaut "loss theory"). Neither the merit of the defense nor the disputed certainty of the amount defeats entitlement once the verdict liquidates the loss as of a prior date — computation is then a purely ministerial/mathematical duty. BARRED / generally unavailable on: unliquidated damages; most tort damages (deemed too speculative to liquidate before judgment); personal-injury non-economic damages (pain and suffering); other non-economic damages; punitive damages; and future/prospective damages (no interest on losses not yet incurred). Personal-injury/tort special rule: prejudgment interest is allowed only on the discrete economic, out-of-pocket components with a fixed date of loss (e.g., paid medical bills, lost wages already incurred), not on the general damages. Where the factfinder does not fix a date of loss and it cannot be determined from the evidence, some DCAs award interest only from the date of the verdict.
accrual: Accrues from the date the plaintiff suffered the pecuniary loss (date of loss). For breach of contract, typically the date payment/performance was due; for qualifying economic tort/out-of-pocket losses, the date the actual loss was incurred. Runs through the date of judgment. If no date of loss is established or determinable, interest may run only from the date of the verdict (per Third DCA and similar authority).
simple_or_compound: Simple. Florida does not compound prejudgment interest; once computed it is added to principal and the total then bears post-judgment interest (avoiding "interest on interest").
confidence_notes: High confidence. Current 8.06% rate confirmed from the official Florida CFO judgment-interest-rates page (effective Jul 1, 2026); rate-setting formula confirmed from Fla. Stat. § 55.03 on the Florida Senate official statute site. Florida has NO statute that separately sets a prejudgment interest rate — entitlement, accrual, and the liquidated/tort restrictions are common-law doctrine from the Florida Supreme Court's Argonaut Ins. Co. v. May Plumbing Co. (1985), which pegs prejudgment interest to the § 55.03 statutory rate from the date of loss. Because § 55.03's rate is reset quarterly by the CFO, the effective prejudgment rate varies over the accrual window; classified as 'variable' rather than a single fixed number. Statute § 687.01 supplies the underlying default legal rate (tied to § 55.03) where a contract specifies no rate. Restrictions verified via the Florida Bar Journal ('Prejudgment and Postjudgment Interest: What's in a Name?'), which restates the controlling caselaw.

rate_basis: Statutory legal rate of interest under OCGA 7-4-2(a)(1)(A): 7% per annum simple interest where no rate is established by written contract. This legal rate governs prejudgment interest on liquidated demands per OCGA 7-4-15. A separate, variable rate (Federal Reserve prime rate + 3%) applies to unliquidated tort damages under the Unliquidated Damages Interest Act, OCGA 51-12-14, but only when a strict written-demand procedure is satisfied.
statute_cite: O.C.G.A. § 7-4-15 (interest runs on liquidated demands) and O.C.G.A. § 7-4-2(a)(1)(A) (7% legal rate); O.C.G.A. § 51-12-14 (Unliquidated Damages Interest Act, prime + 3% for tort unliquidated damages); see also O.C.G.A. § 13-6-13 (interest on damages generally).
formula: Two regimes. (1) LIQUIDATED demands (contract/fixed-and-certain sums): fixed 7% per annum simple, the legal rate under OCGA 7-4-2(a)(1)(A) (applies where no written-contract rate is set); mandatory as a matter of law under OCGA 7-4-15. (2) UNLIQUIDATED tort damages: variable rate = prime rate as published by the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System on the 30th day following mailing of the last written notice, PLUS 3 percent; rate locks on that 30th day (OCGA 51-12-14).
restrictions: Award depends entirely on claim type. LIQUIDATED demands (OCGA 7-4-15): prejudgment interest at 7% is MANDATORY, awarded as a matter of law, when the sum owed is fixed or certain by agreement or otherwise; it is NOT available where a genuine question of fact remains as to the amount owed (then the demand is unliquidated and 7-4-15 interest is not authorized). UNLIQUIDATED damages in TORT (OCGA 51-12-14): recoverable only if the claimant first serves written notice by registered/certified mail or statutory overnight delivery demanding a sum, the defendant fails to pay within 30 days, AND the final judgment is for an amount NOT LESS THAN the demand; statute is in derogation of common law and strictly construed. Under 51-12-14, interest is awarded by the trial court, NOT the jury. General tort/unliquidated claims outside 51-12-14's procedure get no prejudgment interest as of right. Punitive damages and future damages are not a proper base for prejudgment interest.
accrual: LIQUIDATED (7-4-15): interest runs from the time the party became liable and bound to pay (if payable on demand, from the time of demand; for demand promissory notes, from date). UNLIQUIDATED tort (51-12-14): interest begins to run from the 30th day following the date of mailing/delivering the written notice, and runs until the date of judgment.
simple_or_compound: Simple. OCGA 7-4-2 specifies the 7% legal rate as simple interest; Georgia courts hold prejudgment interest is calculated as simple, not compound, interest.
confidence_notes: High confidence. Confirmed exact wording of OCGA 7-4-2(a)(1)(A) ("7 percent per annum simple interest where the rate percent is not established by written contract") via the ga.elaws.us mirror of the official Georgia Code, and cross-checked 7-4-15 and 51-12-14 text across multiple sources (Justia 2024 code, FindLaw, ga.elaws.us). Justia and the Georgia General Assembly site (legis.ga.gov) blocked direct WebFetch (403), so the official-mirror ga.elaws.us was used for verbatim confirmation. Key nuance for the dataset: Georgia has no single prejudgment rate — the DEFAULT a court applies is the 7% fixed legal rate for liquidated demands; the prime+3% variable rate applies only to qualifying unliquidated tort claims under the strict 51-12-14 demand procedure. The 51-12-14 rate is a moving target tied to the Federal Reserve prime rate on the 30th day after notice.

rate_basis: Fixed statutory legal rate of 10% per year under HRS 478-2 (the same rate courts apply as prejudgment interest when awarded under HRS 636-16). Exception: for obligations of the State, the rate is the Wall Street Journal prime rate for each calendar quarter, capped at 10% per year.
statute_cite: HRS 636-16 (award/commencement of prejudgment interest; discretionary); HRS 478-2(1) (10% legal rate applied); HRS 478-3 (10% on judgments); HRS 478-7 (compound interest not recoverable); HRS 661-8 (bar/limit against the State)
formula: Fixed value: 10% per year (simple), applied at the court's discretion. State-debtor exception: variable = WSJ prime rate (posted first business day of the month preceding the calendar quarter) per calendar quarter, but never exceeding 10%/yr.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is DISCRETIONARY, not automatic. HRS 636-16 authorizes the judge to award interest and to designate the commencement date "to conform with the circumstances of each case." It is available in BOTH tort and breach-of-contract cases (unlike many states, Hawaii does not limit prejudgment interest to liquidated/ascertainable contract claims). Hawaii case law treats the award and the commencement date as within the trial court's discretion, primarily to prevent injustice from delay in reaching judgment. Prejudgment interest is barred/limited on claims against the State: under HRS 661-8 the State did not waive sovereign immunity from prejudgment interest except where a contract expressly stipulates for payment of interest (122 Haw. 150, 223 P.3d 215). Note the rate applied when interest IS awarded is the 10% legal rate of HRS 478-2(1); post-judgment interest under HRS 478-3 is NOT allowed to run on the 636-16 prejudgment interest amount (74 Haw. 1, 837 P.2d 1273).
accrual: Discretionary commencement date set by the judge per HRS 636-16. Earliest permissible date: in tort, the date the injury first occurred; in breach of contract, the date the breach first occurred. The court may select a later date to fit the circumstances (e.g., to avoid rewarding a party for delay).
simple_or_compound: Simple. Compound interest is not recoverable in Hawaii (HRS 478-7), and prejudgment interest is not compounded; post-judgment interest is not allowed to accrue on the prejudgment-interest component.
confidence_notes: High confidence. Verbatim statutory text obtained: HRS 478-2, 478-3, and 478-4 from the official Hawaii State Legislature (capitol.hawaii.gov) Chapter 478 PDF (binary read directly); HRS 636-16 text confirmed via FindLaw and search of the capitol.hawaii.gov Chapter 636 PDF. The 10% figure is a fixed statutory legal rate that has not changed. Structure is 'discretionary-with-default': the AWARD and COMMENCEMENT DATE are discretionary under 636-16, but the RATE, when awarded, is the fixed 10% under 478-2(1) (state-debtor exception uses WSJ prime capped at 10%). Official capitol.hawaii.gov section pages (HRS_0636-0016.htm and HRS_0478-0002.htm) return HTTP 403 to automated fetch; verbatim text was instead confirmed from the legislature's own chapter PDFs and the case annotations therein (86 Haw. 21; 74 Haw. 1; 122 Haw. 150).

rate_basis: Statutory legal rate of interest under Idaho Code 28-22-104(1): "twelve cents (12) on the hundred by the year" (i.e., 12% per annum), applied as prejudgment interest when there is no express written contract fixing a different rate. This is a flat statutory rate, NOT the variable post-judgment rate (which is 5% plus the treasurer-set one-year Treasury base rate under 28-22-104(2)).
statute_cite: Idaho Code 28-22-104(1) (legal rate of interest, 12%); prejudgment liquidated/ascertainable requirement per Idaho case law (e.g., Bouten Constr. Co. v. H.F. Magnuson Co., 133 Idaho 756, 992 P.2d 751 (1999); Davis v. Prof'l Bus. Servs., 109 Idaho 810, 712 P.2d 511 (1985))
formula: Fixed value: 12% per annum (Idaho Code 28-22-104(1), "twelve cents on the hundred by the year"). Applies unless an express written contract fixes a different rate, in which case the contract rate governs. This differs from the post-judgment rate, which is a variable formula: 5% + one-year constant-maturity U.S. Treasury base rate set annually July 1 by the Idaho State Treasurer, rounded up to nearest 1/8%.
restrictions: CRITICAL: Prejudgment interest is available ONLY where the claim is liquidated OR the amount is ascertainable by a mere mathematical process. It is BARRED on unliquidated damages. Generally NOT available in tort cases unless the damages are reasonably ascertainable/liquidated (Davis v. Prof'l Bus. Servs.; Bouten Constr. v. H.F. Magnuson). Contract claims qualify when the sum due is fixed or mathematically determinable. If an express written contract sets a different interest rate, that rate applies instead of 12%. The 28-22-104(1) categories eligible for the 12% legal rate include: money due by express contract; money after it becomes due; money lent; money received to the use of another and retained beyond a reasonable time without consent; money due on settlement of mutual accounts from the date the balance is ascertained; money due on open accounts after three months from the last item.
accrual: Interest accrues from the date the money became "due" (for contract, the date of breach), provided that at that point the amount was liquidated or ascertainable by mathematical computation. For settlement of mutual accounts, accrual runs from the date the balance is ascertained; for open accounts, after three months from the date of the last item.
simple_or_compound: simple
confidence_notes: Rate and statutory categories confirmed verbatim from the official Idaho State Legislature statute page for Idaho Code 28-22-104. The 12% figure is the flat statutory "legal rate of interest" that Idaho courts apply as prejudgment interest under 28-22-104(1); it is distinct from and higher than the variable post-judgment rate under 28-22-104(2) (5% + Treasury base). High confidence on the rate (12%) and on the liquidated/ascertainable restriction, which is well-settled Idaho Supreme Court doctrine. Note: some claims may instead carry a contract rate if fixed in writing. Simple (not compound) interest is the standard Idaho application; the statute prescribes a 365-day-year calculation for judgment interest.

current_rate_percent: 6 (personal injury/wrongful death, 735 ILCS 5/2-1303(c)); 5 (Interest Act liquidated/contract claims, 815 ILCS 205/2)
rate_basis: Two separate fixed statutory rates. (1) 6% per annum for personal injury and wrongful death actions under the Prejudgment Interest Act, 735 ILCS 5/2-1303(c), effective July 1, 2021. (2) 5% per annum ("five per centum per annum") for liquidated/ascertainable money claims under the Interest Act, 815 ILCS 205/2 — the long-standing Illinois prejudgment interest rate for written instruments, money lent, settled accounts, and money unreasonably/vexatiously withheld. Illinois has NO single unified prejudgment rate; which applies depends on claim type.
statute_cite: 735 ILCS 5/2-1303(c) (personal injury/wrongful death, 6%); 815 ILCS 205/2 (Interest Act, 5%). Post-judgment default under 735 ILCS 5/2-1303(a) is 9%.
formula: Both are fixed statutory rates, not indexed. PI/wrongful death: 6% per annum on the judgment (minus punitive damages, sanctions, statutory attorney's fees, and statutory costs), accruing from filing, capped at 5 years; if a qualifying written settlement offer exists, interest runs only on the amount by which the judgment exceeds the highest offer. Interest Act: 5% per annum on liquidated/ascertainable sums.
restrictions: CRITICAL claim-type limits. (1) Interest Act (815 ILCS 205/2), 5%: available ONLY where the amount is LIQUIDATED or subject to easy computation — written instruments (bond, bill, promissory note), money lent/advanced, money due on a settled/liquidated account, money received to another's use and retained, and money withheld by "unreasonable and vexatious delay." BARRED on unliquidated damages where the amount is genuinely disputed until judgment (traditional Illinois rule: no prejudgment interest on unliquidated tort/damage claims outside a specific statute). (2) The 6% PI/wrongful-death regime (735 ILCS 5/2-1303(c)) is the statutory EXCEPTION that overrides the no-interest-on-unliquidated-tort rule, but ONLY for personal injury and wrongful death; it EXCLUDES punitive damages, sanctions, statutory attorney's fees, and statutory costs, and does not extend to other tort or property claims. Held constitutional in Cotton v. Coccaro, 2023 IL App (1st) 220298.
accrual: Personal injury/wrongful death (735 ILCS 5/2-1303(c)): accrues from the date the action is FILED (tolled while voluntarily dismissed and refiled), capped at 5 years maximum. Interest Act (815 ILCS 205/2): accrues from the date the money became due / accounts were liquidated and the balance ascertained (or, for vexatious withholding, from the date of the unreasonable delay).
simple_or_compound: Simple interest. 735 ILCS 5/2-1303(c) provides "interest calculated at the rate of 6% per annum" with no compounding language; the Interest Act 815 ILCS 205/2 likewise provides simple 5% per annum.
confidence_notes: High confidence, verified against the official Illinois General Assembly (ilga.gov) statute text for 815 ILCS 205/2 (5% per annum) and multiple sources quoting 735 ILCS 5/2-1303(c) (6% per annum, PI/wrongful death, 5-year cap, accrual from filing, exclusions). The ILGA fulltext.asp URLs 404'd (site restructure) but the current ILGA document URL (081502050K2.htm) resolved. Key nuance: Illinois has no single prejudgment rate — the 6% figure applies ONLY to personal injury/wrongful death (in effect since July 1, 2021, upheld constitutional in Cotton v. Coccaro, 2023 IL App (1st) 220298); the general prejudgment rate for liquidated contract/money claims is 5% under the Interest Act. Post-judgment default (distinct) is 9% under 735 ILCS 5/2-1303(a). Reported current_rate as 6% primary because it is the modern statutory default courts apply in the most common prejudgment-interest litigation (PI/wrongful death); 5% governs liquidated contract claims.

rate_basis: Two distinct regimes. (A) CONTRACT/liquidated money claims: fixed 8% per annum under IC 24-4.6-1-103. (B) TORT claims: discretionary under the Tort Prejudgment Interest Statute (TPIS), IC 34-51-4-9 — court sets a simple rate within a statutory band of not less than 6% and not more than 10% per year (no single fixed rate; court's choice). The "8" reported as current_rate is the fixed contract-claim rate, the only single determinable number; the tort rate is a 6-10% court-set band.
statute_cite: Contract/liquidated: IC 24-4.6-1-103 (8% per annum). Tort: IC 34-51-4-9 (rate, 6%-10% simple), with IC 34-51-4-1 (applies to civil actions arising from tortious conduct), IC 34-51-4-6 (qualified settlement offer prerequisite), IC 34-51-4-8 (accrual/48-month cap). Common-law "Roper rule" (ascertainable-damages requirement) governs contract prejudgment interest.
formula: Contract claims: fixed 8% per year (IC 24-4.6-1-103) — not tied to a published index. Tort claims: no formula/index; the trial court selects a simple rate within the 6%-10% statutory range at its discretion (IC 34-51-4-9).
restrictions: CRITICAL claim-type split. CONTRACT/liquidated money claims (IC 24-4.6-1-103): prejudgment interest at 8% is available AS A MATTER OF RIGHT (not discretionary) only where damages are complete and ascertainable as of a fixed time by fixed rules of evidence/known standards of value (common-law Roper rule) — i.e., liquidated/readily-computable sums (written instruments not specifying a rate, accounts stated, closed accounts, money had and received). Unliquidated damages requiring judgment/discretion to fix the amount do NOT qualify. TORT claims (IC 34-51-4, the TPIS): prejudgment interest is DISCRETIONARY and the TPIS replaced (not merely supplemented) the common-law rule for tort. It is available in personal-injury/property-damage tort actions but is GATED by a qualified-settlement-offer procedure — a plaintiff must have made a qualifying written settlement offer (IC 34-51-4-6), and a defendant's timely qualified offer of settlement (within ~9 months) can BAR any prejudgment interest (IC 34-51-4-5). No prejudgment interest on punitive damages or on future damages. Medical-malpractice claims have special accrual timing tied to the medical review panel.
accrual: Contract (IC 24-4.6-1-103): accrues from the date money became due / date of demand — e.g., date of settlement on money due on a written instrument without a stated rate, or the date an itemized bill was rendered and payment demanded on accounts stated/closed accounts/money had and received. Tort (IC 34-51-4-8): accrues beginning on the LATEST of (1) 15 months after the cause of action accrued, (2) 6 months after the claim is filed (if med-mal provisions IC 34-18-8/9 do not apply), or (3) 180 days after a medical review panel is formed under IC 34-18-10; total accrual period may not exceed 48 months.
simple_or_compound: Simple. Tort statute (IC 34-51-4-9) expressly requires the court to compute prejudgment interest "at the simple rate." Contract-claim prejudgment interest (IC 24-4.6-1-103, 8%) is likewise applied as simple interest.
confidence_notes: High confidence, statutory text confirmed. Verified exact language of IC 34-51-4-9 (simple rate, min 6% / max 10%), IC 34-51-4-8 (accrual dates + 48-month cap), and IC 24-4.6-1-103 (8% per annum from date due/demand) via FindLaw statute mirrors; corroborated by Justia 2024 Indiana Code and Indiana practitioner sources (Barrett McNagny, The Indiana Lawyer DTCI, Pavlack Law) for the Roper-rule ascertainable-damages requirement and the TPIS-replaces-common-law-for-tort holding. NOTE: Indiana has no single unified prejudgment rate — contract/liquidated claims get a fixed 8%; tort claims get a court-set 6%-10% simple rate contingent on the qualified-settlement-offer procedure. The official iga.in.gov statute pages are JavaScript-rendered and could not be read via WebFetch, but FindLaw/Justia mirror the official 2024 Indiana Code text verbatim. official_url points to the iga.in.gov IC 24-4.6-1-103 anchor (contract 8% rate); tort rate is at iga.in.gov IC 34-51-4-9.

rate_basis: 1-year U.S. Treasury constant maturity rate (Federal Reserve H.15 report, settled immediately prior to the date of judgment) plus 2 percentage points. Current computation: 4.06% (H.15 1-yr CMT, July 8, 2026) + 2.00% = 6.06%. The rate is fixed as of the date of judgment and does not float after judgment. The Iowa State Court Administrator distributes notice of the current rate monthly to all district courts.
statute_cite: Iowa Code § 668.13 (esp. subsecs. 1–5); cross-referenced by Iowa Code § 535.3(1)(a)
formula: variable: (1-year Treasury constant maturity from Fed H.15 report settled immediately prior to judgment date) + 2%. Same rate basis as post-judgment (§ 535.3(1)(a) pegs all judgment interest to § 668.13). If the judgment is rendered on a contract fixing the rate, the contract rate applies instead, not exceeding the § 535.2 maximum.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is broadly available on money due on judgments/decrees, including both tort/comparative-fault (§ 668.13 is in the comparative fault chapter) and contract claims (via § 535.3), and does NOT require the claim to be liquidated in the strict sense. KEY EXCLUSIONS: (1) Future damages get NO prejudgment interest — § 668.13(4) states interest on future damages does not begin to accrue until the date of entry of judgment. (2) Punitive/exemplary damages get NO prejudgment interest — Iowa case law (e.g., Vasquez v. LeMars Mut. Ins. Co.) holds punitive damages cannot be ascertained until the trier of fact rules, so prejudgment interest is not allowed on them. (3) Contract-fixed rate governs and caps the award where the judgment is rendered on a contract expressing an interest rate (§ 668.13(2)), not exceeding the § 535.2 usury maximum. In personal-injury cases, past/accrued economic damages accrue prejudgment interest from commencement, but the future-damages component does not until judgment.
accrual: From the date of commencement of the action (i.e., when suit is filed), per § 668.13(1) — EXCEPT interest awarded for future damages, which does not begin to accrue until the date of entry of judgment (§ 668.13(4)).
simple_or_compound: Simple. § 668.13(5) provides interest "shall be computed daily to the date of the payment" — daily simple accrual, not compounding (except structured/periodic non-lump-sum payments under § 668.3(7), which reflect interest per annuity principles).
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence on statutory text, formula, accrual, and simple-interest treatment — verified verbatim from the official Iowa Legislature PDF of Iowa Code 2026 § 668.13 (downloaded and read directly) and § 535.3. The rate is variable; there is no single fixed default number. Current numeric rate (6.06%) is a computed illustration: 1-yr Treasury CMT of 4.06% from the Federal Reserve H.15 release dated July 8, 2026, plus the statutory 2%. The exact rate a court applies depends on the H.15 value settled immediately prior to the specific judgment date and is distributed monthly by the Iowa State Court Administrator; the same rate basis applies to both prejudgment and post-judgment interest. Punitive-damages exclusion is grounded in Iowa Supreme Court case law rather than the statute's plain text. Sources: legis.iowa.gov (§§ 668.13, 535.3), federalreserve.gov H.15.

rate_basis: Statutory legal rate of interest under K.S.A. 16-201(a): 10% per annum when no other rate is agreed upon. This fixed 10% applies to ordinary prejudgment interest on liquidated/ascertainable (typically contract) money claims. NOTE: a SEPARATE, variable prejudgment rate applies to civil tort actions filed on/after July 1, 2023 — 2 percentage points below the K.S.A. 16-204(e)(1) post-judgment rate. That post-judgment rate is 8.25% for July 1, 2025–June 30, 2026 (per Kansas Secretary of State notice), so the current tort prejudgment rate = 8.25% − 2 = 6.25% for that period.
statute_cite: K.S.A. 16-201 (legal/prejudgment rate; tort provision at close of section referencing K.S.A. 16-204(e)(1)); K.S.A. 16-204(e)(1) (post-judgment rate used to derive tort prejudgment rate)
formula: General/liquidated (contract) prejudgment interest: fixed 10% per annum (K.S.A. 16-201(a)). Civil tort actions filed on/after July 1, 2023: variable = [K.S.A. 16-204(e)(1) post-judgment rate] minus 2 percentage points, where the post-judgment rate = 4 percentage points above the NY Federal Reserve discount rate (per Wall Street Journal money rates column) as of July 1 preceding judgment; set annually and published by the Kansas Secretary of State in the Kansas Register (2nd July issue). Post-judgment rate for 7/1/2025–6/30/2026 = 8.25%, so tort prejudgment = 6.25%.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is available only on LIQUIDATED claims — where both the amount due and the date it became due are fixed and certain, or definitely ascertainable by mathematical computation. It is generally BARRED on unliquidated claims (amount not fixed/ascertainable). A good-faith dispute over liability does not by itself defeat prejudgment interest once the claim is liquidated. Even for liquidated claims, the award is a matter of the trial court's DISCRETION (reviewed for abuse of discretion) — but the RATE is not discretionary (fixed 10% for general claims; formula-set for torts). Punitive damages do not bear prejudgment interest. For civil tort actions filed on/after July 1, 2023, a distinct reduced variable rate applies (see formula). No PI-specific statutory carve-out beyond the general liquidated-claim rule.
accrual: Accrues from the date the claim became due / the amount became liquidated (the day the account is liquidated and the balance ascertained; for periodic obligations, from each date the respective amount became due), running until the date of judgment.
simple_or_compound: simple
confidence_notes: High confidence, verified against the official Kansas Revisor of Statutes text (K.S.A. 16-201, 16-204) and the Kansas Secretary of State's Kansas Register notice (Vol. 44, Issue 27, July 3, 2025) confirming the 8.25% post-judgment rate for 7/1/2025–6/30/2026. Kansas has a bifurcated prejudgment regime: (1) a fixed 10% statutory rate for ordinary liquidated/contract claims under 16-201(a) — reported here as the primary current_rate_percent; and (2) a variable rate for civil tort actions filed on/after 7/1/2023 (2 points below the post-judgment rate = 6.25% currently). The 10% figure is the long-standing default a court applies to liquidated money claims; the tort rate is claim-type-specific and derivative. Availability of prejudgment interest is discretionary and limited to liquidated claims per Kansas case law (e.g., Edward Kraemer & Sons v. City of Overland Park), but the applicable rate itself is fixed by statute. Interest is simple (no interest on interest).

rate_basis: Fixed statutory "legal rate of interest" of 8% per annum under KRS 360.010(1). This is the rate for the PREjudgment period. Note: it is distinct from the 6% judgment/post-judgment rate in KRS 360.040(1). The 2017 amendments (2017 Ky. Acts ch. 17) cut the KRS 360.040 judgment rate from 12% to 6% but left the KRS 360.010 legal rate at 8%, so prejudgment interest on liquidated claims still accrues at 8%.
statute_cite: KRS 360.010(1) (8% legal rate governing prejudgment interest); KRS 360.040(1) (6% judgment rate, incl. judgment for prejudgment interest, from entry). Case law: 3D Enterprises Contracting Corp. v. Louisville & Jefferson County Metropolitan Sewer Dist., 174 S.W.3d 440, 450 (Ky. 2005); Nucor Corp. v. Gen. Elec. Co., 812 S.W.2d 136, 141 (Ky. 1991).
formula: Fixed value: 8% per annum (KRS 360.010(1), "the legal rate of interest is eight percent (8%) per annum"). Not indexed/variable for judicial prejudgment purposes. If a written contract specifies a rate, that contract rate governs instead (KRS 360.010(3)-(4), KRS 360.040(3)).
restrictions: CRITICAL SPLIT by claim type. LIQUIDATED / ascertainable claims (a fixed contract price, a bill or note past due, an amount due on open account, a sum "made certain or fixed by agreement or by operation of law"): prejudgment interest is awarded AS A MATTER OF RIGHT at 8% (3D Enterprises; Nucor). UNLIQUIDATED claims (most torts, personal-injury, damages not fixed until the factfinder decides, e.g. pain/suffering, unliquidated breach): prejudgment interest is DISCRETIONARY with the trial court/jury — no default entitlement; often denied absent bad faith/egregious conduct. Contract claims with an agreed rate use the contract rate, not 8% (KRS 360.010(3), 360.040(3)). Special bar: a debt buyer/creditor may NOT recover statutory prejudgment interest on a charged-off consumer account where the original creditor had elected a contract rate above the statutory rate, thereby extinguishing the 8% statutory right (Unifund CCR Partners v. Harrell, 509 S.W.3d 33 (Ky. 2017)). Punitive damages and future damages do not bear prejudgment interest.
accrual: For liquidated claims, prejudgment interest accrues from the date the claim became due/liquidated (e.g., date of breach, date payment was due, or when the sum became fixed — for a charged-off debt, the charge-off date fixes the principal and starts the prejudgment period) up to entry of judgment. For discretionary (unliquidated) awards, the accrual start date is also within the court's discretion. Post-judgment, the 6% KRS 360.040 rate runs from the date the judgment is entered.
simple_or_compound: Prejudgment interest under KRS 360.010 is simple interest (the statute states a flat per-annum legal rate with no compounding provision). By contrast, the KRS 360.040 judgment/post-judgment 6% rate is expressly "compounded annually from the date the judgment is entered."
confidence_notes: High confidence, verified against verbatim primary statutory text pulled from the Kentucky Legislature's official statute site (apps.legislature.ky.gov PDFs). KRS 360.010(1) confirmed: "the legal rate of interest is eight percent (8%) per annum" (effective July 14, 2018). KRS 360.040(1) confirmed: "a judgment, including a judgment for prejudgment interest, shall bear six percent (6%) interest compounded annually from the date the judgment is entered" (effective June 29, 2017). The one interpretive nuance worth flagging: KRS 360.040(1)'s reference to "a judgment for prejudgment interest" means the 6% rate governs the entered judgment (post-judgment accrual), while the 8% KRS 360.010 legal rate governs the prejudgment period itself — this is the settled reading (2017 amendments cut only the judgment rate 12%->6%, leaving the 8% legal rate intact). The liquidated-vs-unliquidated distinction (matter of right vs. discretion) is well-settled Kentucky Supreme Court doctrine (3D Enterprises, Nucor). Law-firm sources used only to locate/confirm; rate figures come from the official statute PDFs at /Users/michaeldube/.claude/projects/-Users-michaeldube-Desktop-Passive-Income-Ideas/2c898f87-82da-476b-b6e4-9e4e8abc01a3/tool-results/webfetch-1783650376435-e6utd9.pdf (KRS 360.010) and webfetch-1783650377532-6mqjxt.pdf (KRS 360.040).

rate_basis: Single statewide "judicial interest" rate set annually by the Louisiana Commissioner of Financial Institutions under La. R.S. 13:4202(B). The 2026 rate is 7.50% per annum (calendar year 2026), confirmed by the Louisiana Office of Financial Institutions (OFI). The same judicial-interest rate applies to both prejudgment and post-judgment interest — Louisiana does not distinguish the two rates. Recent rates: 2025=8.25%, 2024=8.75%, 2023=6.50%, 2022=3.50%.
statute_cite: La. R.S. 13:4202 (rate/formula for "judicial interest" on all sums that are the object of a judicial demand); La. R.S. 13:4203 (legal interest attaches from date of judicial demand in ex delicto/tort cases); La. Civ. Code art. 2000 & La. R.S. 9:3500 (legal interest on contractual sums of money from the time due, at the R.S. 13:4202 rate)
formula: Variable, reset annually. Under R.S. 13:4202(B), on the first business day of October the Commissioner of Financial Institutions ascertains the Federal Reserve Board of Governors' approved "discount rate" (as published in the Wall Street Journal) and adds three and one-quarter (3.25) percentage points. The result is the judicial interest rate for the following calendar year. Published in the Louisiana Register, Louisiana Bar Journal, and designated newspapers. (For Oct 2025 determination, discount rate ~4.25% + 3.25 = 7.50% for CY2026.)
restrictions: Louisiana is notably BROAD/permissive — prejudgment "judicial interest" is available and generally MANDATORY across claim types, unlike most states. TORT (ex delicto): R.S. 13:4203 uses "shall" — legal interest attaches by operation of law from date of judicial demand on ALL judgments sounding in damages ex delicto, INCLUDING unliquidated/damages claims (not barred). Courts and parties cannot alter the start date; it runs automatically. CONTRACT: Civ. Code art. 2000 gives interest on a sum of money from the time it is due (i.e., can run from breach/due date, potentially before judicial demand), at the agreed rate or, absent agreement, the legal (judicial) rate. So no liquidated-only bar and no tort/unliquidated bar as in many states. Limits: prejudgment interest is not awarded on FUTURE damages until they accrue (interest on future/not-yet-incurred damages runs only from the date of the fact-finder's determination/judgment, not from judicial demand); it compensates for loss of use of money and is not awarded on punitive/exemplary sums as if due earlier. Federal-law-based claims heard in LA may follow federal interest rules instead.
accrual: Depends on claim type. TORT (ex delicto): from the date of JUDICIAL DEMAND (filing of suit) per R.S. 13:4203, mandatory. CONTRACT / sum of money: from the time the sum is DUE (which may predate suit) per Civ. Code art. 2000. Future/not-yet-incurred damages: interest runs only from the date those damages are determined (judgment), not from judicial demand.
simple_or_compound: Simple interest (Louisiana judicial interest is computed as simple interest; the statute sets an annual rate applied per annum without compounding).
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence. 2026 rate of 7.50% confirmed directly from the official Louisiana Office of Financial Institutions judicial-interest-rates page (setting agency), which also lists 2025=8.25%, 2024=8.75%, 2023=6.50%, 2022=3.50%. Formula (Fed discount rate + 3.25 pts, set 1st business day of October, effective following calendar year) confirmed via R.S. 13:4202(B) text (FindLaw/legislature references). Accrual rules confirmed from R.S. 13:4203 text (via legis.la.gov d=77703) for tort and Civ. Code art. 2000 for contract. Key nuance: Louisiana uses ONE "judicial interest" rate for both prejudgment and post-judgment, and prejudgment interest is broadly mandatory (available on unliquidated tort damages), which is the opposite of the liquidated-only/tort-barred approach in many states. Justia URLs returned HTTP 403 during fetch, so primary confirmation relied on OFI (official) plus the Louisiana Legislature and FindLaw statutory text. Note the OFI page cite for R.S. 13:4202 is authoritative for the rate; the exact per-year discount-rate arithmetic is inferred but the published 7.50% figure is directly sourced.

rate_basis: One-year U.S. Treasury bill rate plus 3%. The "one-year U.S. Treasury bill rate" is statutorily defined as the weekly average one-year constant maturity Treasury yield published by the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, for the last full week of the calendar year immediately prior to the year in which prejudgment interest begins to accrue. For interest accruing in 2026, the applicable prior-year figure is the last full week of 2025 (Mon Dec 22 - Fri Dec 26, 2025). The Fed H.15 1-year constant maturity daily yields (series RIFLGFCY01) that week were 3.53, 3.52, 3.50, 3.49 (Dec 25 holiday/ND), averaging 3.51%. 3.51% + 3% = 6.51%. (Cross-checked against U.S. Treasury daily par yield curve 1-Yr for the same week: 3.53/3.52/3.50/3.49, also 3.51% average.)
statute_cite: 14 M.R.S. § 1602-B (esp. subsections 3 and 5)
formula: 1-year U.S. Treasury bill rate + 3%, where the T-bill rate = weekly average one-year constant maturity Treasury yield (Federal Reserve Board of Governors) for the last full week of the calendar year immediately before the accrual year. For 2026 accrual: 3.51% (last full week of 2025, Dec 22-26) + 3% = 6.51%. Rate resets annually and is fixed for the life of a given case based on the accrual year. Publisher of the underlying index: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (H.15 Selected Interest Rates / WGS1YR). Statutory fallback: if the Fed ceases publishing the yield, the Maine Supreme Judicial Court sets an approximating rate annually by rule.
restrictions: Maine is unusually broad and does NOT limit prejudgment interest to liquidated/ascertainable claims. Under sub 3, prejudgment interest is allowed in essentially ALL civil actions other than small claims (sub 1) and interest-bearing contract/note actions (sub 2) - this expressly INCLUDES tort and unliquidated damages (e.g., personal injury). No liquidated-only, contract-only, or ascertainable-amount requirement. Exceptions/limits: (a) Small claims actions get NO prejudgment interest unless the rate is based on a contract or note (sub 1). (b) Civil/small-claims actions involving a contract or note with an interest provision use the CONTRACT/NOTE rate, not the Treasury formula (sub 2). (c) Prejudgment interest may NOT be added to the judgment amount when determining the base on which post-judgment interest accrues (sub 6). (d) The trial court MAY, on petition of the nonprevailing party for good cause, fully or partially WAIVE the interest (sub 5). No separate special PI-only rule; PI is covered by the general sub 3 rate. Punitive/future damages are not separately addressed or excluded in the statute (courts generally include the full damages award, but no explicit statutory carve-out or inclusion for punitive/future damages).
accrual: Accrues from the time a sworn notice of claim setting forth the cause of action is served on the defendant personally or by registered/certified mail, until the date judgment is entered. If no notice of claim was given, accrues from the date the complaint is filed. (In contract/note-interest cases, the rate is fixed as of the notice-of-claim date, or complaint-filing date if no notice given.) Interest is SUSPENDED for the duration of any continuance exceeding 30 days that the prevailing party requests and obtains. (14 M.R.S. § 1602-B(5).)
simple_or_compound: Simple. The statute prescribes a flat rate applied to the damages amount with no compounding; sub 6 further bars prejudgment interest from being folded into the base for post-judgment interest.
confidence_notes: High confidence. Full statutory text extracted verbatim from the Maine Legislature's official PDF (14 M.R.S. sec 1602-B, generated 10.20.2025, current through Oct 1, 2025). Rate structure (T-bill + 3%) and the definition tying it to the Fed weekly average one-year constant maturity yield for the last full week of the prior calendar year are confirmed. The 6.51% figure is my computation from the authoritative Federal Reserve H.15 daily 1-year constant maturity series (RIFLGFCY01) for the last full Mon-Fri week of 2025 (Dec 22-26 = 3.53/3.52/3.50/3.49, avg 3.51%), plus the statutory 3%, and independently cross-checked against U.S. Treasury's daily par yield curve for the same week (also 3.51% avg). FRED's automated WGS1YR CSV endpoint returned 403 in this environment, so the published rounded weekly figure was reconstructed from primary daily H.15 data rather than read from FRED's weekly row; the Fed constructs WGS1YR as exactly this business-day average, so the values agree. Note the rate is case-specific: it is set by the accrual year, so a case whose interest began accruing in an earlier year uses that year's prior-year figure, not 6.51%. Sub 7 gives a legacy rate scheme (8% under $30k; T-bill+1% over $30k) only for interest that began accruing before July 1, 2003.

rate_basis: Constitutional legal rate of interest (Md. Const. Art. III, sec. 57 = 6% per annum), applied by Maryland courts as the default prejudgment interest rate. This is DISTINCT from Maryland's 10% post-judgment rate (Cts. & Jud. Proc. sec. 11-107). Where a contract specifies its own interest rate, that contract rate governs prejudgment interest instead of 6%.
statute_cite: Md. Const. Art. III, sec. 57 (6% legal rate); prejudgment-interest entitlement/restrictions are common-law, governed by Buxton v. Buxton, 363 Md. 634, 770 A.2d 152 (2001), and I.W. Berman Props. v. Porter Bros., 276 Md. 1, 344 A.2d 65 (1975). Post-judgment rate (10%, distinct) is Cts. & Jud. Proc. sec. 11-107.
formula: Fixed 6% per annum (constitutional legal rate), unless the underlying written contract specifies a different interest rate, in which case the contract rate applies. Not a published/variable index rate.
restrictions: Availability depends on claim type under the three-category common-law framework (Buxton v. Buxton): (1) AS OF RIGHT (mandatory) where "the obligation to pay and the amount due had become certain, definite, and liquidated by a specific date prior to judgment" — e.g., written contracts to pay money on a day certain (bills of exchange, promissory notes), sums payable under leases as rent, bonds, and readily-ascertainable conversion claims. (2) BARRED (prohibited) in tort cases where recovery is for bodily harm, emotional distress, or similar intangible/unliquidated damages "not easily susceptible of precise measurement" (the award is presumed comprehensive); in such personal-injury/wrongful-death torts, interest runs only from the date of the verdict, not before (Taylor v. Wahby). (3) DISCRETIONARY for the broad middle category of contract cases where damages are not fixed/liquidated — award is within the discretion of the trier of fact. So: liquidated/ascertainable claims and written contracts to pay money get it as of right; unliquidated tort/intangible claims are barred; other contract claims are discretionary.
accrual: For "as of right" liquidated claims: from the date the sum became due/certain (the specific date prior to judgment on which the fixed amount was owed and withheld). For contract claims generally: from the date of breach/when payment was due. For barred tort/PI claims: no prejudgment interest — interest runs only from the date of verdict.
simple_or_compound: Simple. The Court of Appeals in Buxton (following Md. Nat'l Bank v. Cummins, 322 Md. 570) limited prejudgment interest to simple interest at 6%.
confidence_notes: High confidence. Verified against primary sources: the official Maryland General Assembly Constitution (Art. III, sec. 57 = 6% legal rate) and the full text of the Maryland Court of Appeals opinion in Buxton v. Buxton, 363 Md. 634, 770 A.2d 152 (2001), read directly. Key nuance: prejudgment interest in Maryland is governed by COMMON LAW, not by statute (sec. 11-107 sets only the 10% POST-judgment rate). The default prejudgment rate courts apply is the 6% constitutional legal rate (simple), UNLESS a contract specifies its own rate. rate_kind is "discretionary-with-default" because entitlement turns on the three-category test (as-of-right for liquidated claims, barred for intangible torts, discretionary for other contracts), but a determinable default rate (6%) exists whenever interest is awarded. Statute cite for the rate is the Constitution; entitlement/restrictions are case-law (Buxton; I.W. Berman; First Virginia Bank v. Settles, 322 Md. 555; State Hwy. Admin. v. Kim, 353 Md. 313).

rate_basis: Statutory fixed rate of 12% per annum, set directly by M.G.L. c. 231, §§ 6B, 6C, and 6H. Not tied to any index; the legislature fixed it at "twelve per cent per annum." In contract actions the contract rate governs if one is established; the 12% applies as the default when no contract rate is set.
statute_cite: M.G.L. c. 231, § 6B (tort/personal injury/property damage); § 6C (contract); § 6H (catch-all for damages where interest not otherwise provided by law)
formula: Fixed 12% per annum (simple). Contract actions under § 6C: use the contract rate if one is established, otherwise 12% per annum. Same 12% rate applies both pre- and post-judgment under this statutory scheme.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is mandatory and added automatically by the clerk (not discretionary) once damages are awarded. § 6B covers pecuniary damages for personal injuries, consequential damages, and property damage in tort actions. § 6C covers actions based on contractual obligations. § 6H is a catch-all adding § 6B's rate to any damages award where interest is not otherwise provided by law. Prejudgment interest is added even if it pushes the award beyond a statutory liability cap. Key exclusions/nuances: PUNITIVE damages do not bear prejudgment interest (interest runs on compensatory pecuniary damages only; punitive/multiple-damage enhancements are excluded). Interest is computed on the damages actually awarded, not on future/post-judgment components. For claims against the Commonwealth, a different rate applies under § 6I (not 12%). Where a specific statute sets its own interest treatment (e.g., certain wage/ch. 93A matters), that governs over these general provisions.
accrual: Tort (§ 6B): from the date of commencement of the action (filing). Contract (§ 6C): from the date of the breach or demand; if that date cannot be established, from the date of commencement of the action. Catch-all (§ 6H): from the date of commencement of the action.
simple_or_compound: Simple
confidence_notes: High confidence. Verified from the official Massachusetts Legislature site (malegislature.gov) for §§ 6B, 6C, and 6H. Rate is a fixed statutory 12% per annum, simple interest (well-settled in MA practice; the statutes set a flat annual rate with no compounding). The Massachusetts SJC recently reaffirmed the 12% statutory rate. Same 12% rate applies pre- and post-judgment under this scheme, so it could also be characterized as same-as-postjudgment; classified here as "fixed" because the number is set directly in the prejudgment statutes. Note: § 6I sets a different (variable, Treasury-based) rate for judgments against the Commonwealth; § 6C allows an established contract rate to override the 12% default.

rate_basis: 1% plus the average high yield paid at auctions of 5-year U.S. Treasury Notes during the 6 months immediately preceding July 1 and January 1, as certified by the Michigan State Treasurer (MCL 600.6013(8)). For the period beginning January 1, 2026 the certified 5-year Treasury note yield was 3.725%, so 3.725% + 1% = 4.725%. The rate re-sets every January 1 and July 1.
statute_cite: MCL 600.6013 (esp. subsections (8) general/tort default rate, (7) written-instrument rate capped at 13%, (6) future damages, (5)/(9) tort settlement-offer adjustments); wrongful-death/other special rates at MCL 600.6455
formula: Variable, published by the Michigan State Treasurer. General/default rate = 1% + average 5-yr U.S. Treasury note auction yield for the preceding 6 months, re-certified each Jan 1 and Jul 1, compounded annually. Current (eff. 2026-01-01): 3.725% + 1% = 4.725%. Exception: judgment on a written instrument that specifies a legal interest rate uses that instrument's rate, capped at 13%/yr (subsection (7)).
restrictions: Michigan has NO separate general prejudgment-interest statute; instead MCL 600.6013 (nominally the "interest on money judgments" statute) runs interest FROM THE DATE THE COMPLAINT IS FILED, so the complaint-to-judgment portion functions as statutory prejudgment interest. It is available broadly and is NOT limited to liquidated/ascertainable claims — it applies to tort AND contract, and to unliquidated/tort damages (e.g., personal injury). Key limits: (1) Written-instrument claims (e.g., promissory notes) use the instrument's specified rate instead, capped at 13%/yr — subsection (7). (2) FUTURE DAMAGES: interest on the future-damages portion of a judgment runs only from the date of filing to the date of judgment entry (not to satisfaction) and is limited — subsection (6); no prejudgment interest accrues on damages that had not yet been incurred. (3) Tort settlement-offer mechanics: if the judgment debtor failed to make a bona fide reasonable written settlement offer, interest runs from complaint filing; a defendant's rejected reasonable offer can CUT OFF interest as of the offer's filing date (subsection (9)), while a plaintiff's rejected reasonable offer can INCREASE the rate by 2% from the rejection date (subsection (5)/(13)).
