(4) Costs and attorney fees generally do not bear this interest. Punitive/exemplary damages are not a standard Michigan category, so no special punitive rule.
accrual: From the date the complaint is filed (for complaints filed on/after Jan 1, 1987) to the date of satisfaction of the judgment — MCL 600.6013(8). Exception for future damages: interest runs from filing only to the date of judgment (subsection (6)). Written-instrument interest under subsection (7) likewise accrues from date of filing the complaint.
simple_or_compound: Compound — compounded annually (MCL 600.6013(8); the written-instrument rate under (7) is also compounded annually)
confidence_notes: High confidence. Rate mechanism and accrual confirmed directly from the Michigan Legislature statute text (MCL 600.6013). Current 4.725% figure = certified 3.725% avg 5-yr Treasury yield for the 6 months preceding Jan 1, 2026, +1%, corroborated by two independent sources (Michigan Treasury/courts certification and a judgment-interest calculator). The Jan 1, 2026 rate governs complaints/periods in the first half of 2026; the rate re-sets Jul 1, 2026 (that new certification was not yet published as of the search date, 2026-07). Note the Michigan-specific framing: this is technically the money-judgment interest statute, but because interest runs from the date of filing the complaint it serves as Michigan's de facto prejudgment interest for both tort and contract. The Michigan.gov Treasury page (michigan.gov/taxes/interest-rates-for-money-judgments) returned 403 to automated fetch but its rate content is reflected in the courts publication and search extracts.

rate_basis: Tiered. For judgments/awards NOT over $50,000: a variable rate = the one-year constant maturity Treasury yield (latest Federal Reserve statistical release), rounded to nearest 1%, OR 4%, whichever is greater (4% floor). The State Court Administrator sets this annually by Dec. 20 for the following calendar year. For calendar year 2026 that rate is 4% (also 4% for 2025; 5% for 2024). SEPARATE FLAT TIER: for judgments/awards OVER $50,000 (excluding those for/against the state or a political subdivision, and family-court actions), the rate is a flat 10% per year until paid — this over-$50k 10% tier is not indexed and does not change year to year.
statute_cite: Minn. Stat. § 549.09, subd. 1(b) (preverdict/preaward/prereport interest on pecuniary damages) and subd. 1(c)(1)-(2) (rate)
formula: Two tiers. (1) Judgments/awards not over $50,000: rate = max(one-year constant maturity Treasury yield rounded to nearest 1%, 4%). Set annually by the State Court Administrator by Dec. 20 (official annual notice at https://www.revisor.mn.gov/court_rules/rule/msinte/). 2026 = 4%; 2025 = 4%; 2024 = 5%. (2) Judgments/awards over $50,000 (other than for/against the state or a political subdivision, or family-court actions): flat 10% per year until paid.
restrictions: Preverdict interest is allowed broadly on "pecuniary damages" — it is NOT limited to liquidated or contract claims and DOES apply to tort/personal-injury claims (for past pecuniary damages), even when damages were unliquidated/not readily ascertainable (Minnesota abolished the old ascertainability limit in 1984). BARRED / excluded from preverdict interest under subd. 1(b): (1) future damages; (2) punitive damages, fines, or other noncompensatory damages; (3) workers' compensation judgments/awards/benefits (except third-party actions); (4) judgments/awards not in excess of the conciliation-court amount in § 491A.01 (small-claims threshold); (5) the portion of any verdict/award founded on interest, costs, disbursements, attorney fees, or similar items. OFFER-OF-SETTLEMENT LIMIT: if the party awarded interest offered a written settlement and the opposing party made a written offer closer to (or exceeding) the verdict, the court adjusts interest accordingly — a prevailing party whose recovery does not beat the opponent's written offer can lose preverdict interest from the date of that offer. No special PI-only rate; PI past-damages get the standard tiered rate above.
accrual: Accrues from the earliest of: (a) commencement of the action, (b) a demand for arbitration, or (c) the time of a written notice of claim — whichever occurs first. To use the written-notice-of-claim date, the notice must contain sufficient information/demand AND the action must be commenced within two years of that written notice; otherwise interest accrues only from commencement of the action.
simple_or_compound: Simple. Subd. 1(c)(1) expressly specifies "simple interest per annum."
confidence_notes: High confidence, verified against primary sources: Minnesota Revisor of Statutes official text of § 549.09 (https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/549.09) and the Revisor's official annual court-rules notice of interest rates (https://www.revisor.mn.gov/court_rules/rule/msinte/), which states the calendar-year 2026 rate is 4% (2025 = 4%, 2024 = 5%) and confirms the flat 10% tier for judgments over $50,000. The 4% figure is the variable/small-judgment tier and functions as the current default preverdict rate; note the parallel flat 10% tier applies once the judgment/award exceeds $50,000. Restrictions (future/punitive/noncompensatory/workers-comp/small-claims exclusions, accrual, simple interest, offer-of-settlement adjustment) all taken verbatim from subd. 1(b)-(c). The mncourts.gov PDF returned HTTP 403, but the equivalent official Revisor court-rules page corroborated the same rates.

rate_basis: Miss. Code Ann. § 75-17-7 lets the trial judge set the per annum prejudgment-interest rate at their discretion. For judgments founded on a sale or contract, the rate follows the contract rate. Where no contract rate is specified (or for non-contract liquidated claims), courts apply the statutory legal rate of 8% per annum under Miss. Code Ann. § 75-17-1. 8% is the fallback default a court applies absent a contractual rate.
statute_cite: Miss. Code Ann. § 75-17-7 (interest on judgments/decrees; prejudgment rate set by judge, contract judgments bear contract rate); Miss. Code Ann. § 75-17-1 (legal rate of interest = 8% per annum where none specified). Key case law: Arcadia Farms P'ship v. Audubon Ins. Co., 77 So.3d 100 (Miss. 2012); Coho Resources, Inc. v. McCarthy, 829 So.2d 1 (Miss. 2002).
formula: Discretionary per-annum rate set by the judge. Contract/sale judgments: same rate as the contract. If no contract rate is specified, the statutory legal rate of 8% per annum (Miss. Code Ann. § 75-17-1) applies as the default. Not a published/indexed variable rate — no periodic Treasury or agency reset.
restrictions: CRITICAL: Prejudgment interest is allowed ONLY on liquidated / reasonably ascertainable amounts. Mississippi Supreme Court holds NO prejudgment interest is allowed where the amount owed is UNLIQUIDATED prior to judgment. This BARS prejudgment interest on most tort / personal-injury claims and medical-malpractice claims because damages there are typically unliquidated (Coho Resources v. McCarthy — personal injury; med-mal damages generally not liquidated). Available for contract claims and other liquidated/sum-certain claims. Even when available it is DISCRETIONARY with the trial court (§ 75-17-7 — 'a per annum rate set by the judge'). Punitive damages and unliquidated future damages do not bear prejudgment interest.
accrual: For judgments founded on a sale or contract: interest runs at the contract rate and, per Arcadia Farms P'ship v. Audubon Ins. Co. (2012), the trial court MAY award it from the DATE OF BREACH (not limited to the post-complaint period). For 'all other' judgments under § 75-17-7: accrual date is determined by the judge to be fair but IN NO EVENT PRIOR TO THE FILING OF THE COMPLAINT.
simple_or_compound: Simple interest (statute does not authorize compounding; the § 75-17-1 legal rate is computed by the actuarial method but prejudgment interest is applied as simple interest).
confidence_notes: High confidence on statute and structure. Exact verbatim text of Miss. Code Ann. § 75-17-7 confirmed via multiple sources (identical wording): contract judgments bear the contract rate; all other judgments bear a per-annum rate set by the judge, accruing no earlier than filing of the complaint. § 75-17-1 legal rate of 8% per annum confirmed as the default where no rate is specified. Justia and legalfix pages returned HTTP 403 to WebFetch, but the statutory language and the 8% legal rate were corroborated across Justia search snippets, FindLaw, USLegal, and law-firm treatments. Case-law restrictions (unliquidated bar; tort/PI/med-mal exclusion; date-of-breach accrual for contract) confirmed via Butler Snow (Arcadia Farms) and Kellum Law Firm (Coho Resources). rate_kind = discretionary-with-default because the rate is judge-set but 8% is the court-applied statutory fallback. NOTE: § 75-17-7 governs BOTH prejudgment and post-judgment interest — Mississippi uses the same statute/mechanism for both, so the prejudgment default (8% / contract rate) mirrors the post-judgment default.

rate_basis: Two tracks. TORT (RSMo 408.040): prejudgment interest accrues at the intended Federal Funds Rate (as established by the Federal Reserve Board) + 5%. As of July 2026 the intended/target Fed Funds Rate is 3.50%-3.75% (upper limit 3.75%), so the current tort prejudgment rate = 3.75 + 5 = 8.75%. CONTRACT/liquidated (RSMo 408.020): a fixed 9% per annum legal rate applies when no rate is agreed upon, for money due on written contracts and on accounts after due and demand. The 8.75% figure reported is the tort-track variable rate; the contract track is a flat 9%.
statute_cite: Mo. Rev. Stat. (RSMo) § 408.040 (tort prejudgment interest); Mo. Rev. Stat. § 408.020 (9% legal rate on contracts/liquidated claims)
formula: TORT (variable): intended Federal Funds Rate (Federal Reserve Board) + 5.00%. Current = 3.75% (target-range upper limit, July 2026) + 5% = 8.75%. Note a distinct sub-rule: a judgment FOR prejudgment interest, once entered, bears interest at Fed Funds + 3% (that is the rate on the interest-judgment going forward, NOT the accrual rate on the tort claim). CONTRACT (fixed): 9.00% flat under § 408.020.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is NOT freely available; it is claim-type restricted. (1) TORT actions (§ 408.040.2): available ONLY if the claimant made a written demand/settlement offer sent by certified mail return receipt, accompanied by a signed affidavit describing the claim, injuries, and a computation of damage categories with supporting documentation (medical provider list, bills, employer list and authorizations for PI/wrongful-death wage claims), the demand references § 408.040 and stays open 90 days, the suit is FILED within 120 days of the demand (else prejudgment interest is forfeited), and the final judgment/order EXCEEDS the demand. If the judgment does not exceed the demand, no prejudgment interest. (2) CONTRACT/liquidated claims: under § 408.020 and Missouri common law, prejudgment interest is allowed as of right only on LIQUIDATED or readily ascertainable demands (sums certain, definite by computation on written contracts or on accounts after demand). Unliquidated damages that cannot be ascertained by fixed standards generally do NOT bear § 408.020 prejudgment interest (the tort demand-letter route under § 408.040 is the mechanism for unliquidated tort claims). Punitive damages and future damages are not a proper base for prejudgment interest.
accrual: TORT (§ 408.040.2): accrues from a date 90 days AFTER the demand/offer was received (per certified-mail return receipt), OR from the date the demand/offer was rejected without a counteroffer, whichever is earlier. CONTRACT (§ 408.020): accrues from the date the money became due and payable on a written contract, or on an account after it is due and demand of payment is made.
simple_or_compound: Simple. The statute states a per annum rate and does not authorize compounding; Missouri courts apply § 408.020 and § 408.040 prejudgment interest as simple interest.
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence, verified against the official Missouri Revisor of Statutes (revisor.mo.gov) full text of § 408.040 and § 408.020. Key nuance verified twice: for TORT actions the prejudgment interest ACCRUES at intended Fed Funds Rate + 5% (same formula the statute uses for judgments) — NOT +3%. The "+3%" figure in the statute is a separate, narrower rule for the rate a judgment FOR prejudgment interest bears once entered, and it is easy to misread as the accrual rate (an early search snippet did misstate it as the accrual rate). Current intended/target Federal Funds Rate confirmed at 3.50%-3.75% (upper limit 3.75%) as of July 2026 via Federal Reserve/FRED, giving a current tort prejudgment rate of 8.75%. Because the tort rate floats with the Fed Funds Rate, it is VARIABLE and must be recomputed as the Fed changes rates. The § 408.040 tort scheme was amended effective Jan. 2015 (H.B. 1231 / related bills) to replace the former flat 9% prejudgment tort rate with the Fed-Funds-plus-5% formula and the strict demand-letter/120-day-filing prerequisites. Reported current_rate_percent = 8.75 reflects the tort track; the alternative contract/liquidated track under § 408.020 is a flat 9%.

rate_basis: Montana's statutory legal rate of interest. For ordinary (non-tort) prejudgment interest on liquidated/ascertainable claims, MCA 27-1-211 grants a MANDATORY right to interest but sets no rate itself; the applicable rate is the legal rate under MCA 31-1-106 = 10% per year (fixed), unless a written contract, or a law/ordinance/resolution of a public body, fixes a different rate. SEPARATE SPECIAL RULE for tort/personal-injury actions: MCA 27-1-210 uses a VARIABLE rate = the Federal Reserve H.15 bank prime loan rate + 3%, set as of January 1 each year and held through December 31. Prime was 6.75% on Jan 1, 2026 (cut to 6.75% effective Dec 10, 2025), so the 2026 tort prejudgment rate = 9.75%. (Note: MCA 25-9-205, also prime+3%, governs POST-judgment interest, not prejudgment.)
statute_cite: MCA 27-1-211 (right to prejudgment interest on liquidated/ascertainable claims) + MCA 31-1-106 (legal rate = 10%/yr); MCA 27-1-212 (discretionary interest for non-contract breach / oppression, fraud, malice); MCA 27-1-210 (special tort/injury prejudgment interest, prime+3%). Cf. MCA 25-9-205 (post-judgment interest, prime+3%).
formula: Non-tort/liquidated claims: fixed 10% per year (legal rate, MCA 31-1-106), unless a written contract or public-body rate applies. Tort/personal-injury (MCA 27-1-210): variable = H.15 bank prime loan rate + 3%, set as of Jan 1 each year (2026 = 6.75% + 3% = 9.75%).
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is MANDATORY (not discretionary) under MCA 27-1-211 only when three criteria are met: (1) an underlying monetary obligation exists; (2) the amount of recovery is certain or capable of being made certain by calculation (liquidated/ascertainable); and (3) the right to recover vests on a particular day. Unliquidated/uncertain claims generally do NOT get mandatory prejudgment interest under 27-1-211. For breach of a non-contract obligation and every case of oppression, fraud, or malice, interest is DISCRETIONARY with the jury (MCA 27-1-212), and 27-1-212 does not apply to claims against governmental entities under the Montana Tort Claims Act. TORT/PERSONAL-INJURY actions have their own regime (MCA 27-1-210): interest runs only on the portion of damages capable of being made certain, and is EXPRESSLY BARRED on pain and suffering, mental anguish/suffering, injury to credit/reputation/financial standing, loss of established way of life, loss of consortium, exemplary/punitive damages, attorney fees, and on future damages until they are actually incurred.
accrual: Non-tort/liquidated (MCA 27-1-211): interest runs from the day the right to recover vests (the day the sum became due/certain), except during any time the debtor is prevented by law or by the creditor's act from paying. Tort/personal-injury (MCA 27-1-210): interest runs from the date 30 days after the claimant presents a written statement to the opposing party stating the claim and how the specific sum was calculated.
simple_or_compound: Simple. MCA 31-1-106 legal-rate prejudgment interest is applied as simple interest under Montana practice; MCA 27-1-210 tort interest and MCA 25-9-205 both expressly state interest may not be compounded.
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence, verified against primary sources (Montana Legislature official code site mca.legmt.gov and Federal Reserve H.15). Key nuance: the primary/default prejudgment rate a Montana court applies for ordinary liquidated claims is the 10% fixed legal rate (MCA 31-1-106 via the mandatory right in MCA 27-1-211) — this is distinct from post-judgment interest (MCA 25-9-205), which was amended to prime+3%. A common error is to report the prime+3% figure as the prejudgment rate: that variable formula is correct for TORT/PERSONAL-INJURY prejudgment interest (MCA 27-1-210 = 9.75% for 2026, prime 6.75% + 3%) and for post-judgment interest, but NOT for ordinary contract/liquidated prejudgment interest, which remains the fixed 10% legal rate. Bank prime loan rate confirmed at 6.75% as of Jan 1, 2026 (cut to 6.75% effective Dec 10, 2025). Because Montana has both a fixed default (10%) and a variable tort-specific rate, I set rate_kind=fixed and current_rate_percent=10 to reflect the general default, with the tort variable rate documented in rate_basis/formula/restrictions.

rate_basis: Two parallel/independent statutory tracks. (A) LIQUIDATED claims (Neb. Rev. Stat. sec. 45-103.02(2)) and CONTRACT-based claims (sec. 45-104) accrue at a FIXED 12% per annum "as provided in section 45-104" ("Unless otherwise agreed, interest shall be allowed at the rate of twelve percent per annum"). This 12% is a matter of right and is the primary default a court applies. (B) UNLIQUIDATED claims (sec. 45-103.02(1)) accrue "interest as provided in section 45-103" — i.e., the VARIABLE state judgment rate (2 percentage points above the bond investment yield of the first-of-quarter 26-week U.S. Treasury bill auction). That variable rate is currently 5.723% (effective April 16, 2026; scheduled to change to 5.970% effective July 16, 2026), set/published by the Nebraska State Court Administrator (Judicial Branch). Rate depends entirely on claim type.
statute_cite: Neb. Rev. Stat. sec. 45-104 (12% contract/liquidated rate); sec. 45-103.02 (prejudgment interest; accrual; conditions — subsec. (1) unliquidated uses sec. 45-103 rate, subsec. (2) liquidated uses sec. 45-104 rate); sec. 45-103.04 (exceptions); sec. 45-103 (variable judgment-rate formula for unliquidated claims)
formula: Two rates by claim type: (1) LIQUIDATED and CONTRACT claims = FIXED 12% per annum (sec. 45-104), unless the parties agreed to another rate. (2) UNLIQUIDATED claims = variable judgment rate per sec. 45-103 = bond investment yield of the first auction of each annual quarter of the 26-week U.S. Treasury bill (published by U.S. Secretary of the Treasury) + 2 percentage points, in effect on date of judgment entry; distributed by the Nebraska State Court Administrator. Current sec. 45-103 rate: 5.723% (eff. 2026-04-16), changing to 5.970% (eff. 2026-07-16). See https://nebraskajudicial.gov/rules/administrative-policies-schedules/judgment-interest-rate
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is NOT freely available on all claims — Nebraska splits by claim type. LIQUIDATED claims (no reasonable controversy as to right to recover or amount; computable with exactness without opinion/discretion) get 12% as a matter of right from when the cause of action arose (sec. 45-103.02(2)). CONTRACT-based claims in four sec. 45-104 categories (money due on a written instrument; settled account from date balance agreed; money received to another's use and retained without consent; money loaned or due and withheld by unreasonable delay) get 12%; per Weyh v. Gottsch (Neb. 2019), sec. 45-104 is an independent track that reaches these contract claims WITHOUT requiring the claim be liquidated. UNLIQUIDATED claims (including tort) get prejudgment interest ONLY under sec. 45-103.02(1) and only if ALL strict conditions are met: plaintiff made a written settlement offer by certified mail (return receipt), the offer was exceeded by the judgment, offer made at least 10 days before trial, copy + proof of delivery filed with the clerk, and offer not accepted before trial or within 30 days (whichever first); interest then runs only from the date of that first offer, not from the injury. BARRED entirely (sec. 45-103.04) on: any action arising under Chapter 42 (domestic relations/family law), and any action against the State, a political subdivision, or their employees for a negligent or wrongful act within scope of employment. Punitive damages: Nebraska constitutionally does not permit punitive damages, so no prejudgment interest on them. No special personal-injury carve-out beyond the general unliquidated-claim offer-of-settlement mechanism.
accrual: Liquidated claims (sec. 45-103.02(2)) and contract claims (sec. 45-104): from the date the cause of action arose. Unliquidated claims (sec. 45-103.02(1)): from the date of the plaintiff's first qualifying offer of settlement that is later exceeded by the judgment — NOT from the date of loss/injury. Both run until entry of judgment.
simple_or_compound: Simple. Nebraska statutes and case law treat statutory prejudgment interest as simple interest (no statutory provision for compounding; interest is computed on the unpaid balance/principal until entry of judgment).
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence, verified against primary/official sources: nebraskalegislature.gov statute pages for sec. 45-104 (fixed 12%), sec. 45-103.02 (subsec. (1) references sec. 45-103 variable rate for unliquidated; subsec. (2) references sec. 45-104 12% for liquidated), and sec. 45-103.04 (exceptions: Chapter 42 actions and state/subdivision/employee negligence). The current variable sec. 45-103 rate (5.723% eff. 2026-04-16; 5.970% eff. 2026-07-16) confirmed on the official Nebraska Judicial Branch judgment-interest-rate page. I set current_rate_percent = 12 because that fixed rate is what a court applies as a matter of right to the core liquidated/contract prejudgment-interest cases; the unliquidated-claim path instead borrows the variable sec. 45-103 rate (5.723% as of today) and is gated on the strict offer-of-settlement conditions. Weyh v. Gottsch, 303 Neb. 280 (2019) confirms sec. 45-104 and sec. 45-103.02 are alternate, independent recovery tracks. Simple (not compound) is inferred from absence of any compounding provision and standard Nebraska practice; statute does not use the word "simple" expressly.

rate_basis: Prime rate at the largest bank in Nevada, as ascertained by the Commissioner of Financial Institutions (Financial Institutions Division), plus 2 percentage points. Current prime rate ascertained for July 1, 2026 = 6.75%, so the rate is 6.75% + 2% = 8.75% for the period July 1 - Dec 31, 2026. (Jan 1 - Jun 30, 2026 was also 8.75% [prime 6.75% + 2%]; Jul 1 - Dec 31, 2025 was 9.50% [prime 7.50% + 2%]; Jan 1 - Jun 30, 2025 was 10.00% [prime 8.00% + 2%].)
statute_cite: NRS 99.040 (general legal/prejudgment rate on money due when no written contract fixes a different rate); NRS 17.130(2) (interest on the money judgment, expressly running from service of the summons and complaint — i.e., the prejudgment-through-postjudgment period). Both use the same formula: Nevada largest-bank prime rate + 2%.
official_url: https://www.leg.state.nv.us/NRS/NRS-099.html (NRS 99.040); https://www.leg.state.nv.us/nrs/nrs-017.html (NRS 17.130); official current prime rate PDF: https://fid.nv.gov/uploadedFiles/fidnvgov/content/Resources/Prime%20Interest%20Rate%20July%201,%202026.pdf ; FID rate page: https://fid.nv.gov/Resources/Fees_and_Prime_Interest_Rate/
formula: Variable formula, published by the Nevada Commissioner of Financial Institutions (Financial Institutions Division): rate = (prime rate at the largest bank in Nevada on the immediately preceding Jan 1 or Jul 1) + 2 percentage points. FID publishes the ascertained prime rate semiannually. As of Jul 1, 2026 the ascertained prime rate is 6.75%, giving a 8.75% rate. The rate is set by the Jan 1 / Jul 1 preceding the transaction (NRS 99.040) or preceding the date of judgment (NRS 17.130), then adjusted on each subsequent Jan 1 and Jul 1 until satisfied.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest in Nevada is generally limited to LIQUIDATED / readily ASCERTAINABLE sums. NRS 99.040 grants interest on: (a) contracts express or implied (other than book accounts); (b) settlement of book or store accounts from the day the balance is ascertained; (c) money received to the use/benefit of another and detained without consent; (d) unpaid wages/salary after demand. For OPEN/STORE accounts, interest may be awarded only by a court in an action on the debt (AG Op. 98-20). In tort/personal-injury and other unliquidated-damage cases, common-law prejudgment interest generally is NOT available on damages that are not fixed or ascertainable until the fact-finder decides them; Nevada case law (e.g., Ramada / M.C. Multi-Family; Uniroyal Goodrich; Lee v. Ball) allows prejudgment interest only from the date the amount becomes ascertainable or from a valid offer of judgment / date sums were due, not on speculative or FUTURE damages. Under NRS 17.130(2), FUTURE damages draw interest only from entry of judgment (no prejudgment interest on future-damages component). Prejudgment interest is not awarded on punitive/exemplary damages. NRS 42.005 caps and separately treats punitive damages. Offer-of-judgment interest is governed by NRS 17.115 / NRCP 68 mechanics.
accrual: Under NRS 17.130(2), interest on the money judgment (the prejudgment-to-postjudgment period) runs from the time of SERVICE OF THE SUMMONS AND COMPLAINT until satisfied — except any amount representing FUTURE damages, which draws interest only from entry of judgment. Under NRS 99.040, contractual/statutory interest on money due accrues "from the time it becomes due" (for book/store accounts, from the day the balance is ascertained; for wages, after demand). Where damages are unliquidated, courts run prejudgment interest from the date the amount became ascertainable.
simple_or_compound: Simple interest. The FID's official prime-rate publication (citing NRS 99.040 / AG Op. 98-20) states "Simple interest may be imposed at the rate established in NRS 99.040." Nevada courts compute NRS 17.130 / 99.040 interest as simple, not compound.
confidence_notes: High confidence. Rate and formula confirmed directly from official primary sources: NRS 99.040 and NRS 17.130 text on the Nevada Legislature site (leg.state.nv.us), and the current ascertained prime rate from the Nevada Financial Institutions Division (fid.nv.gov) official PDF, which lists July 1, 2026 = 6.75% (extracted from the FID PDF). 6.75% + 2% = 8.75% current statutory rate. Cross-checked against the Washoe County District Court legal-interest-rate page. Note Nevada is a same-rate jurisdiction: prejudgment and post-judgment interest both use largest-bank prime + 2% (NRS 17.130 uniquely provides the judgment rate applies retroactively from service of summons, functioning as the prejudgment rate on ascertainable/liquidated claims). Claim-type restrictions on prejudgment interest (liquidated/ascertainable requirement; no prejudgment interest on future damages or punitive damages) derive from NRS 17.130(2) text plus Nevada Supreme Court case law; those case-law nuances were characterized from the statute and secondary summaries rather than reading each opinion in full.

rate_basis: Annual simple rate set by the NH State Treasurer under RSA 336:1, II = prevailing discount rate on 26-week U.S. Treasury bills at the last auction before the last day of September, plus 2 percentage points, rounded to the nearest tenth. 2026 = 5.7% (2025 = 6.2%; 2024 = 7.3%). Same rate governs both prejudgment and post-judgment interest.
statute_cite: RSA 336:1, II (rate); RSA 524:1-a (accrual for debt/account/liquidated); RSA 524:1-b (accrual for all other civil pecuniary damages, incl. tort/PI/wrongful death)
formula: 26-week U.S. Treasury bill discount rate at last auction preceding Sept 30 + 2 percentage points, rounded to nearest 0.1%; State Treasurer determines by Dec 1, effective Jan 1–Dec 31 of following year. Published annually by NH State Treasury (treasury.nh.gov/transparency/interest-rates-judgments) and NH Judicial Branch. Same rate as post-judgment interest (single statutory rate under RSA 336:1, II).
restrictions: NH is unusually broad: prejudgment interest is added by statute to essentially ALL pecuniary damage awards, NOT limited to liquidated or contract claims. RSA 524:1-b expressly extends it to "all other civil proceedings" awarding pecuniary damages — personal injury, wrongful death, consequential damages, property/business/reputation damage, and "any other type of loss for which damages are recognized" — so tort and unliquidated claims DO receive prejudgment interest (unlike many states). It is added forthwith by the clerk (mandatory, not discretionary) even if it pushes the judgment beyond a statutory liability cap. Note: applies to compensatory/pecuniary damages found by the court; future damages are effectively excluded because interest runs only up to date of judgment (not on post-judgment/future losses).
accrual: Two tracks. RSA 524:1-a: for an action on a debt, account stated, or where liquidated damages are sought, interest runs from the institution of suit (absent a pre-suit demand; inapplicable if the party pays the money into court under superior court rules). RSA 524:1-b: for all other civil pecuniary-damage awards (tort, PI, wrongful death, property, etc.), interest runs from the date of the writ or the filing of the petition to the date of judgment.
simple_or_compound: simple (RSA 336:1, II expressly specifies the "annual simple rate of interest")
confidence_notes: High confidence. Statute text (RSA 336:1, II; RSA 524:1-a; RSA 524:1-b) retrieved verbatim from the official NH General Court site (gc.nh.gov/rsa). The 2026 rate of 5.7% is confirmed consistently across multiple searches of the primary sources — the NH Judicial Branch (courts.nh.gov/our-courts/superior-court/civil/civil-interest-rates) and the setting agency, NH State Treasury (treasury.nh.gov/transparency/interest-rates-judgments) — with corroborating recent-year values (2025: 6.2%, 2024: 7.3%). Both gov pages block direct automated fetch (Akamai 403), so the rate value was confirmed via search-engine extraction of those official pages rather than a raw page pull; the statute pages themselves were read in full. NH uses one statutory rate for both prejudgment and post-judgment interest (rate_kind logged as variable; it is also effectively same-as-postjudgment).

rate_basis: Rule-based prejudgment interest in tort actions equals the post-judgment rate (R. 4:42-11(b): "interest rates in tort actions after January 1, 1988 are the same as the post-judgment interest rates"). For calendar year 2026 the base rate is 4.5% for judgments not exceeding the Special Civil Part monetary limit ($20,000). For judgments exceeding $20,000, add 2.0% per R. 4:42-11(a)(iii), giving 6.5% for 2026. The base rate equals the average rate of return of the NJ Cash Management Fund for the preceding fiscal year (ending June 30), rounded to the nearest whole or half percent, floor of 0.25%.
statute_cite: N.J. Ct. R. 4:42-11(b) (tort prejudgment interest); rate set by R. 4:42-11(a)(ii)-(iii)
formula: Same as post-judgment rate. Base rate = average rate of return of the NJ Cash Management Fund for the preceding fiscal year (ending June 30), rounded to the nearest whole or one-half percent, not less than 0.25% (R. 4:42-11(a)(ii)). If the judgment exceeds the Special Civil Part monetary limit (currently $20,000), add 2.0% (R. 4:42-11(a)(iii)). 2026 base = 4.5%; over-$20,000 = 6.5%. (Pre-Jan-1-1988 periods: fixed 12%.)
restrictions: Rule 4:42-11(b) prejudgment interest is MANDATORY in TORT actions, including products liability actions ("the court shall... include in the judgment simple interest"). CRITICAL EXCLUSION: prejudgment interest is NOT allowed on any recovery for FUTURE economic losses. In exceptional cases the court may suspend the running of prejudgment interest. Contingent attorney fees are not computed on the interest. The Rule does NOT govern CONTRACT / liquidated / debt claims — for those, prejudgment interest is awarded as a matter of the trial court's EQUITABLE DISCRETION under New Jersey common law (courts often, by convention, apply the same R. 4:42-11 rate but are not bound to). Punitive/exemplary damages do not bear prejudgment interest. Personal-injury tort claims are covered by 4:42-11(b) like other torts, with the future-economic-loss carve-out being the key PI nuance.
accrual: From the date of institution of the action, OR from a date 6 months after the date the cause of action arises, whichever is LATER (R. 4:42-11(b)). Court may suspend the running in exceptional cases.
simple_or_compound: simple
confidence_notes: High confidence. Rate confirmed from the official NJ Courts "Post-Judgment and Pre-Judgment Interest Rates" table (revised January 23, 2026), which lists 2026 = 4.5% and explicitly states "Pre-Judgment: Pursuant to R. 4:42-11(b), interest rates in tort actions after January 1, 1988 are the same as the post-judgment interest rates," plus the +2.0% add-on note for judgments over the $20,000 Special Civil Part limit. 2026 post-judgment rate (4.5% / 6.5%) independently confirmed via the official NJ Courts 2026 notice. Rule 4:42-11(b) text (accrual, simple interest, future-economic-loss exclusion, exceptional-case suspension) confirmed from published rule text. Note the entered current_rate_percent (4.5) is the standard base rate; larger judgments (>$20,000) carry 6.5% for 2026. The important distinction: R. 4:42-11(b)'s mandatory prejudgment interest applies to TORT claims; prejudgment interest on contract/liquidated claims is discretionary equitable relief under common law, not fixed by this Rule.

rate_basis: Fixed statutory ceilings, not indexed. Two tracks: (1) NMSA 1978 Sec. 56-8-4(B) — discretionary prejudgment interest of UP TO 10% per year, awarded at the court's discretion from the date the complaint is served (applies to unliquidated claims, including tort). (2) NMSA 1978 Sec. 56-8-3 — prejudgment interest as a MATTER OF RIGHT at not more than 15% per year on liquidated/ascertainable sums (money due by contract; money received to the use of another and retained without owner's consent; money due upon settlement of matured accounts from the day the balance is ascertained). The 10% figure is the discretionary default ceiling most courts apply to unliquidated claims; 15% is the as-of-right rate for definite contract sums.
statute_cite: NMSA 1978, Sec. 56-8-4(B) (discretionary prejudgment interest, up to 10% from service of complaint); NMSA 1978, Sec. 56-8-3 (prejudgment interest as a matter of right, up to 15%, on liquidated/ascertainable amounts)
formula: Fixed statutory ceilings: 10% max under Sec. 56-8-4(B) (discretionary); 15% max under Sec. 56-8-3 (as of right for liquidated sums). Not tied to any published/floating index and not tied to the post-judgment rate.
restrictions: CRITICAL two-track distinction. (A) MATTER OF RIGHT (Sec. 56-8-3, up to 15%): available only when the claim is LIQUIDATED / ascertainable with reasonable certainty by a mathematical standard fixed in the contract or by established market prices — i.e., money due by contract for a definite sum, money received to the use of another and wrongfully retained, or money due upon settlement of matured accounts. (B) DISCRETIONARY (Sec. 56-8-4(B), up to 10%): for UNLIQUIDATED claims, including tort/personal-injury damages and other amounts not fixed by a mathematical standard; the court MAY (not must) award it and weighs equitable factors — whether plaintiff caused unreasonable delay in adjudication, and whether defendant made a reasonable and timely settlement offer (factors are non-exclusive). Prejudgment interest under 56-8-4(B) is compensatory (to make the victim whole), NOT punitive — it does not attach to punitive-damages awards. Sec. 56-8-4(D): the State of New Mexico and its political subdivisions are EXEMPT except as otherwise provided by statute or common law. Child-support judgments are excluded from the 56-8-4(B) discretionary provision. Note: the 8.75% / 15% rates in Sec. 56-8-4(A) are POST-judgment (money-judgment) rates, not prejudgment.
accrual: Discretionary track (56-8-4(B)): from the date the complaint is served upon the defendant. As-of-right track (56-8-3): from the date the sum became due/ascertainable — e.g., money due by contract accrues from when payment was due; matured accounts accrue from the day the balance is ascertained.
simple_or_compound: Simple interest (statute specifies a per-annum rate with no compounding provision; New Mexico prejudgment interest is applied as simple interest).
confidence_notes: High confidence on statutory text and structure, confirmed against FindLaw reproductions of NMSA 1978 Sec. 56-8-3 and 56-8-4 and NM case-law summaries (the liquidated-vs-unliquidated / matter-of-right-vs-discretionary distinction is well settled, e.g., the ascertainable-with-reasonable-certainty standard). Justia (law.justia.com) returned HTTP 403 during fetch, so I relied on FindLaw's codes.findlaw.com text plus corroborating search extracts; the substantive numbers (10% discretionary cap under 56-8-4(B), 15% as-of-right cap under 56-8-3, 8.75%/15% post-judgment under 56-8-4(A), state exemption under 56-8-4(D)) are consistent across all sources. rate_kind set to discretionary-with-default because the primary prejudgment mechanism (56-8-4(B)) is discretionary but carries a definite statutory ceiling a court applies; verified=true because a determinable statutory rate/ceiling exists (not purely open-ended). The 'current_rate_percent' of 10 reflects the 56-8-4(B) discretionary ceiling; note the separate 15% as-of-right ceiling for liquidated contract sums under 56-8-3.

rate_basis: Statutory legal rate fixed by CPLR 5004(a): "Interest shall be at the rate of nine per centum per annum, except where otherwise provided by statute." Exception: for an action arising out of a consumer debt where a natural person is the defendant, the rate is 2% per annum (CPLR 5004(b), added by L.2021 ch.593). The 9% figure has been unchanged for decades (in effect since 1981) and is not indexed/variable.
statute_cite: N.Y. C.P.L.R. 5004 (rate); C.P.L.R. 5001 (entitlement, accrual, discretion in equity); C.P.L.R. 5002 (interest from verdict to judgment)
formula: Fixed value: 9% per annum simple (2% per annum for consumer-debt actions against a natural person under CPLR 5004(b)). Not a formula and not tied to any published index.
restrictions: Prejudgment (pre-verdict) interest under CPLR 5001 is available AS OF RIGHT only for (1) breach of contract and (2) an "act or omission depriving or otherwise interfering with title to, or possession or enjoyment of, property" (i.e., property-damage/conversion/many economic torts). It is thus effectively limited to liquidated or ascertainable pecuniary damages. In actions "of an equitable nature," interest and its rate/accrual date are DISCRETIONARY with the court (CPLR 5001(a)). CRITICAL BARS: No prejudgment (pre-verdict) interest is allowed on personal-injury or wrongful-death pain-and-suffering / non-economic (unliquidated) damages — those get interest only from the date of the verdict/decision forward under CPLR 5002, not back to the injury. Punitive damages likewise carry no prejudgment interest. (A long-pending legislative proposal to add pre-verdict interest for "bodily injury" actions has NOT been enacted as of this date.) Personal-injury economic components and the whole class of tort recoveries for personal injury remain outside CPLR 5001's "as of right" categories.
accrual: Interest is computed "from the earliest ascertainable date the cause of action existed" (CPLR 5001(b)); for damages incurred later, from the date incurred; where damages arose at various times, interest may be computed on each item from its date or on all damages from "a single reasonable intermediate date." The verdict/report/decision must specify the date from which interest runs. (For equitable actions, the accrual date is set at the court's discretion.)
simple_or_compound: simple
confidence_notes: High confidence. Rate, entitlement, accrual, and consumer-debt exception all confirmed verbatim from the official New York State Senate OpenLegislation source (nysenate.gov) for CPLR 5004, 5001, and 5002. The 9% simple statutory rate is well-settled New York law (unchanged since 1981); the 2% consumer-debt rate stems from L.2021 ch.593. The tort/pain-and-suffering and punitive-damages bars are established via CPLR 5001's "as of right" categories being limited to contract and property claims, with unliquidated personal-injury awards accruing interest only from verdict under CPLR 5002. Simple (non-compounding) interest is the settled rule under Article 50. Only nuance flagged: proposed CPLR 5001 amendments to add pre-verdict interest in bodily-injury actions remain unenacted as of 2026-07-09.

rate_basis: Statutory legal rate of interest fixed at 8% per annum under N.C.G.S. 24-1. This same "legal rate" governs prejudgment interest under N.C.G.S. 24-5 for both contract awards (absent a contract rate) and the compensatory-damages portion of non-contract (tort) awards. It is a flat statutory rate, not indexed to any market benchmark.
statute_cite: N.C. Gen. Stat. 24-5 (Interest on judgments); rate set by N.C. Gen. Stat. 24-1 (Legal rate is eight percent)
formula: Fixed value: 8% per annum (statutory legal rate under G.S. 24-1). Not a formula/index. Note: for contract actions the parties' agreed contract rate applies instead of 8% if the contract so provides (capped at the lower of legal or contract rate for personal/family/household/agricultural consumer credit); for tort/non-contract actions the rate is always the 8% legal rate.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is claim-type-restricted, not universal. CONTRACT actions (G.S. 24-5(a)): the amount awarded on the contract bears prejudgment interest from date of breach; the fact finder must separate principal from interest. Excludes penal bonds (G.S. 24-5(a1)), which bear interest only from entry of judgment. NON-CONTRACT / TORT actions (G.S. 24-5(b)): ONLY the portion of the money judgment the fact finder designates as COMPENSATORY DAMAGES bears prejudgment interest. Any OTHER portion of a tort judgment (e.g., PUNITIVE damages, and non-compensatory components) is BARRED from prejudgment interest and bears interest only from the date of entry of judgment. NC courts do not require the claim to be liquidated/ascertainable for tort compensatory damages to earn statutory prejudgment interest — the statute grants it on the compensatory portion by operation of law (jury need only designate compensatory amount). Personal-injury note: PI compensatory awards qualify under (b) and accrue from commencement of the action (not date of injury); where a liability insurer is involved, the insurer's obligation to pay this prejudgment interest is generally limited by policy coverage. It is a statutory entitlement, not discretionary.
accrual: Contract actions: from the DATE OF BREACH (G.S. 24-5(a)). Non-contract/tort actions: the compensatory-damages portion accrues from the DATE THE ACTION IS COMMENCED (filing), not the date of injury/loss, until the judgment is satisfied (G.S. 24-5(b)). Non-compensatory tort portions and penal bonds accrue only from date of entry of judgment under Rule 58.
simple_or_compound: Simple (statutory legal rate applied per annum to the principal; no compounding provided by statute).
confidence_notes: High confidence. Verbatim text obtained from official NC General Assembly PDFs. G.S. 24-1 (2016-90, s.18(a)) fixes the legal rate at 8% per annum and is confirmed current in the 2025 codification. G.S. 24-5 (last amended 2003-59, s.4) governs prejudgment interest and expressly ties non-contract awards to "the legal rate" (=8%). The critical, well-settled nuances: (1) tort prejudgment interest attaches ONLY to the fact-finder-designated compensatory portion and accrues from commencement of the action, not date of injury; (2) punitive/non-compensatory portions and penal bonds are excluded from prejudgment interest; (3) contract prejudgment interest runs from date of breach and may be at an agreed contract rate. Statute does not authorize compounding, so simple interest applies.

rate_basis: Statutory legal rate of interest fixed at 6% per annum (N.D.C.C. § 47-14-05), applied as the prejudgment interest rate through N.D.C.C. §§ 32-03-04 and 32-03-05. For contract claims, the contracted-for written rate applies if one exists; otherwise the 6% default legal rate. This is DISTINCT from ND's post-judgment rate, which is set annually by the State Court Administrator at prime (first Monday in December) + 3 percentage points under N.D.C.C. § 28-20-34 and is 11.00% for judgments entered in 2025 and 2026 — that variable rate does NOT apply to prejudgment interest.
statute_cite: N.D.C.C. § 32-03-04 (interest on damages certain / liquidated — mandatory); N.D.C.C. § 32-03-05 (discretionary interest on non-contract / tort / oppression, fraud, malice); rate set by N.D.C.C. § 47-14-05 (legal rate 6%). Post-judgment rate (not applicable here) is N.D.C.C. § 28-20-34.
formula: Fixed 6% per annum legal rate (N.D.C.C. § 47-14-05), unless a written contract specifies a different (higher, up to usury ceiling) rate, in which case the contract rate governs. Not a published/variable index — this is a static statutory rate, unchanged and unrelated to the annually-published post-judgment rate.
restrictions: Two distinct tracks. (1) MANDATORY prejudgment interest as of right under § 32-03-04 ONLY for damages that are "certain or capable of being made certain by calculation" and where the right to recover is vested on a "particular day" — i.e., liquidated / readily ascertainable claims (typically contract debts and sums calculable by fixed standards). Recovery of prejudgment interest is precluded on unliquidated damages that are not ascertainable by calculation. (2) DISCRETIONARY prejudgment interest under § 32-03-05 for breach of an obligation NOT arising from contract (tort) and in every case of oppression, fraud, or malice — "interest may be given in the discretion of the court or jury." Personal-injury / tort damages therefore get prejudgment interest only at the trier of fact's discretion, not as of right. Prejudgment interest is NOT allowed on FUTURE damages (they do not become due until judgment). Exemplary/punitive damages carry no prejudgment interest (§ 32-03-35: damages in the chapter are exclusive of exemplary damages and interest except where expressly mentioned). Per § 32-03-06, accepting payment of the whole principal waives all claim to interest unless interest is expressly provided in the contract.
accrual: Under § 32-03-04, interest accrues from the "particular day" the right to recover vested — i.e., the date the liquidated/ascertainable debt became due or the date of breach for contract claims. Under § 32-03-05 (discretionary/tort), the trier of fact fixes the period; interest runs on past damages from when they were incurred/became due up to judgment, but not on future damages. Interest runs up to entry of judgment (post-judgment interest under § 28-20-34 takes over thereafter).
simple_or_compound: Simple. The 6% legal rate under § 47-14-05 is simple interest; North Dakota law generally prohibits compounding of interest (see § 47-14-09, interest may not be compounded), and prejudgment interest is computed as simple interest.
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence, verified against primary/official source. Exact statutory text of N.D.C.C. §§ 32-03-04, 32-03-05 (and surrounding chapter) and § 47-14-05 pulled verbatim from the official ND Legislative Branch (ndlegis.gov) chapter PDFs. § 47-14-05 fixes the legal rate at "six percent per annum unless a different rate ... is contracted for in writing." § 32-03-04 makes interest mandatory on damages "certain or capable of being made certain by calculation" vested on a "particular day"; § 32-03-05 makes it discretionary for non-contract obligations and oppression/fraud/malice. KEY CAUTION for the dataset: do not confuse this with ND's post-judgment rate — that is a separate, variable, annually-published rate (prime + 3 pts = 11.00% for 2025 and 2026 per ndcourts.gov) under § 28-20-34 and does NOT govern prejudgment interest. Prejudgment interest is the static 6% legal rate. Rate is current as of the as-of date; § 47-14-05's 6% figure is long-standing and not subject to annual adjustment.

rate_basis: Statutory rate set annually by the Ohio Tax Commissioner under ORC 5703.47: the federal short-term rate (avg. market yield on U.S. marketable obligations with remaining maturity of 3 years or less, per IRC 26 U.S.C. 1274) for July, rounded to the nearest whole percent, plus 3%. For calendar year 2026 the certified rate is 7% per annum. This same statutory rate applies to both prejudgment and post-judgment interest under ORC 1343.03(A), unless a written contract specifies a different rate.
statute_cite: Ohio Rev. Code 1343.03 (rate in subsection (A); tort discretionary prejudgment interest in subsection (C)); rate set per Ohio Rev. Code 5703.47
formula: Federal short-term rate (IRC 1274) for July, rounded to nearest whole percent, plus 3% = statutory rate for the following calendar year (ORC 5703.47). Same as post-judgment rate. 2026 rate = 7%.
restrictions: Two distinct tracks. (1) CONTRACT / LIQUIDATED claims under ORC 1343.03(A): prejudgment interest is a matter of RIGHT (not discretionary) on money due and payable upon a written contract, book account, settlement, or other instrument of writing — the creditor is entitled to interest at the 1343.03(A) statutory rate (or the contract rate if the written contract provides one). Ohio case law (Royal Elec. Constr. Corp. v. Ohio State Univ.) extends 1343.03(A) prejudgment interest even to unliquidated contract-based claims once judgment fixes the amount. (2) TORT claims under ORC 1343.03(C): prejudgment interest is DISCRETIONARY — awarded only on a party's post-verdict motion (within 14 days of the verdict) and only if the court finds the payor failed to make a good-faith effort to settle AND the payee did NOT fail to make a good-faith effort to settle. NO prejudgment interest on the FUTURE-damages portion of a tort award (1343.03(C)(2)). Division (B)/(D) carve-outs: does not apply where a different interest period is specified by law, in actions against the state in the Court of Claims, or in workers' comp actions under Chapter 4123. Punitive damages: prejudgment interest generally not recoverable on punitive/exemplary awards.
accrual: Contract/liquidated (1343.03(A)): interest accrues from the date the money became due and payable (e.g., breach/when payment was owed). Tort (1343.03(C)(1)): if awarded, interest is computed from the date the cause of action accrued (for cases of admitted/deliberate liability) or, otherwise, from the earlier of (a) the date the plaintiff gave the defendant written notice in person or by certified mail of the claim or (b) the date the complaint was filed, running to the date the judgment is paid.
simple_or_compound: Simple interest.
confidence_notes: High confidence. Primary sources: ORC 1343.03 and ORC 5703.47 text from the official Ohio Laws site (codes.ohio.gov). The 2026 certified rate of 7% per annum (federal short-term rate rounded + 3% under 5703.47) is consistent across sources; the Ohio Department of Taxation official Interest Rates page (tax.ohio.gov/business/additional-resources-section/resource-04) publishes the annual certified rate but returned a 404 on direct fetch at time of check — the 7% figure was corroborated via multiple secondary sources reciting the Tax Commissioner's certification. Ohio applies ONE statutory rate to both prejudgment and post-judgment interest under 1343.03(A); hence rate_kind = same-as-postjudgment. The critical nuance is claim-type: contract/liquidated prejudgment interest is mandatory as of right, while TORT prejudgment interest is discretionary and gated on the good-faith-settlement finding under 1343.03(C).

rate_basis: Average U.S. Treasury Bill rate of the preceding calendar year, certified by the State Treasurer to the Administrative Director of the Courts on the first regular business day in January; the Administrative Director publishes the resulting rate each year. For calendar year 2026 the certified prejudgment rate is 4.13% (applicable to actions filed on or after Jan 1, 2010). (Post-judgment rate for 2026 is 8.75%, set differently under subsection D — the two are NOT the same.)
statute_cite: 12 O.S. Sec. 727.1 (esp. subsecs. E, F, G, I) for personal-injury/personal-rights verdicts; 23 O.S. Sec. 6 for liquidated/ascertainable (contract) claims
formula: Variable, reset annually. Prejudgment rate = average U.S. Treasury Bill rate of the preceding calendar year, certified by the State Treasurer per 12 O.S. 727.1(I) and published by the Administrative Director of the Courts. 2026 published prejudgment rate = 4.13%. The rate applied is the one in effect for the year 24 months after suit was filed, then re-set each succeeding calendar year at the then-current certified rate until judgment is filed. NOTE: distinct from the post-judgment rate (2026 = 8.75%), so NOT same-as-postjudgment.
restrictions: Sharply restricted and split across two statutes. (1) 12 O.S. 727.1(E) authorizes prejudgment interest ONLY on a "verdict for damages by reason of personal injuries or injury to personal rights" (e.g., bodily restraint, personal insult, defamation, invasion of privacy, injury to personal relations) — accepted on/after Nov 1, 2009. It does NOT cover contract, property, or general commercial claims. Future damages: Oklahoma courts have held prejudgment interest runs on past damages only, not on amounts awarded for future/prospective loss (interest is compensation for delayed past loss). Punitive damages: subsection G limits punitive-award interest to accrue only from the date judgment is rendered/filed (no prejudgment period). Government defendants: subsection F caps liability under the Governmental Tort Claims Act and accrues from date suit commenced (no 24-month delay). (2) Liquidated / ascertainable-amount claims (including breach-of-contract debts) are NOT covered by 727.1 — they get prejudgment interest under 23 O.S. 6, which allows interest at the legal rate on damages "certain, or capable of being made certain by calculation," from the day the right to recover vested. Unliquidated tort damages outside the personal-injury/personal-rights category generally get NO prejudgment interest.
accrual: Depends on claim type. For personal-injury/personal-rights verdicts (12 O.S. 727.1(E)): accrual begins the date 24 MONTHS AFTER the suit resulting in the judgment was commenced (not the date of injury), running until verdict acceptance/judgment. For government-entity claims (subsec. F): from the date suit was commenced. For liquidated/contract claims (23 O.S. 6): from the date the right to recover became vested (i.e., when the amount became certain/ascertainable).
simple_or_compound: Simple. Oklahoma prejudgment interest is computed as simple interest on the principal/verdict amount; the statute prescribes annual re-setting of the rate but does not compound accrued interest.
confidence_notes: High confidence. The 2026 prejudgment rate of 4.13% and post-judgment rate of 8.75% come from the official OSCN "Interest on Judgments (2026)" notice (Administrative Director of the Courts, dated Jan 2, 2026, CiteID=551111). Statutory framework confirmed from 12 O.S. 727.1 text (subsecs. E/F/G/I via ok.elaws.us mirror and Justia) and 23 O.S. 6. Two caveats: (a) law.justia (Title 12 detail page and Title 23) returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch, but subsection content was corroborated via the elaws mirror and search snippets; (b) the exact "legal rate" applied under 23 O.S. 6 for liquidated claims is, per Oklahoma case law, tied to the same certified statutory rate, but practitioners should verify the precise rate for a given liquidated claim against current case law. The headline default number a court applies for the covered (personal-injury/personal-rights) prejudgment category in 2026 is 4.13%.

rate_basis: Fixed statutory legal rate of 9% per annum set by ORS 82.010(1). This is the same rate that applies to post-judgment interest under ORS 82.010(2), but prejudgment interest is authorized as interest on "all moneys after they become due" under ORS 82.010(1)(a), not by the judgment subsection.
statute_cite: ORS 82.010(1)(a) (legal rate of interest, 9% on all moneys after they become due); the ascertainability restriction is a judicial gloss (see, e.g., Oregon Supreme Court/Court of Appeals cases requiring liquidated or ascertainable damages). Post-judgment rate parallel: ORS 82.010(2).
formula: Fixed value: 9% per annum (simple). Not indexed or published periodically. Set directly by statute ORS 82.010(1).
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is NOT automatically available on all claims. It runs on "all moneys after they become due" (ORS 82.010(1)(a)) and Oregon courts have restricted it to claims where (1) the exact amount of damages is ASCERTAINED or ASCERTAINABLE by simple computation or by reference to generally recognized standards, AND (2) the time from which interest runs (when the money became due) is easily ascertained. Classic use: liquidated contract/debt claims, money had and received, open accounts. BARRED on unliquidated damages that require jury discretion to fix (e.g., general/non-economic damages, pain and suffering, most personal-injury tort damages, punitive damages, future damages). "Readily ascertainable" does not mean without complication and can precede actual ascertainment, but genuinely unliquidated tort damages do not qualify. Special sub-rate: for judgments in medical/nursing professional-negligence actions, the rate is the lesser of 5% or 3% over the Federal Reserve discount rate (ORS 82.010(2)) — this affects the judgment rate in those cases.
accrual: Interest accrues from the date the money became due / the loss was sustained, i.e., when the ascertainable sum first became payable. For open accounts, from the date of the last item. For services (quantum meruit), from the date service was rendered. The accrual date must itself be readily ascertainable for prejudgment interest to be awarded.
simple_or_compound: Simple interest. ORS 82.010 provides simple interest (subsection (2)(b) expressly makes judgment interest simple unless a contract provides otherwise; the (1) legal rate is likewise applied as simple interest per annum).
confidence_notes: High confidence on the 9% fixed rate — confirmed directly from ORS 82.010(1) text via the Oregon statute source. The rate is a fixed statutory value (not a variable/indexed rate) and has stood at 9% for many years. Coded rate_kind as "fixed" because the numeric prejudgment rate is fixed at 9%; note it also coincidentally equals the general post-judgment rate. The critical nuance is the ASCERTAINABILITY/liquidated-claim restriction, which is judge-made law layered on the statute (well established in Oregon appellate decisions and OSB PLF CLE materials), not spelled out as a limit in the statute itself — so which claims qualify is fact-specific and litigated. Medical/nursing professional-negligence special sub-rate under (2) is a judgment-rate rule but noted for completeness. Official primary source: Oregon Legislature ORS 82.010 (oregonlegislature.gov) and oregon.public.law mirror of the ORS text.

rate_basis: Statutory legal rate of interest, fixed at 6% per annum for the general (contract/non-tort) prejudgment case. NOTE: Pennsylvania is bifurcated — a DIFFERENT regime governs tort/personal-injury cases (see restrictions/formula).
statute_cite: 41 P.S. Sec. 202 (legal rate of interest = 6%); Pa.R.C.P. No. 238 (delay damages in bodily injury/death/property-damage tort actions)
formula: Two regimes. (1) CONTRACT / non-tort: fixed 6% per annum simple under 41 P.S. Sec. 202 (default "legal rate" when no contract rate is specified; a higher agreed contract rate controls if pleaded). (2) TORT (bodily injury, death, property damage) under Pa.R.C.P. 238: VARIABLE rate = the prime rate as listed in the first edition of the Wall Street Journal published for each calendar year for which damages are awarded, PLUS one percent (prime + 1%), not compounded, computed year-by-year.
restrictions: Highly claim-type dependent. CONTRACT: prejudgment interest is awarded AS OF RIGHT only when damages are liquidated/ascertainable (e.g., a sum certain / definite invoice amount due at breach) at 6% under 41 P.S. Sec. 202; for unliquidated contract damages the award of prejudgment interest is DISCRETIONARY with the trial court (Pa. courts, Restatement (Second) of Contracts Sec. 354). TORT: ordinary prejudgment interest is generally NOT available as such; instead Pa.R.C.P. 238 "delay damages" apply and ONLY to actions for bodily injury, death, or property damage. Rule 238 EXCLUDES the portion of the award for future damages, delay damages already added, and punitive damages from the calculation base, and is reduced/tolled by qualifying settlement offers the plaintiff fails to beat by more than 125%. Rule 238 does NOT apply to eminent domain or where delay damages are otherwise allowable by law. Punitive damages get no prejudgment interest/delay damages.
accrual: CONTRACT (liquidated): from the date payment was due / the money became owed (e.g., invoice due date or date of breach). TORT (Rule 238): from a date ONE YEAR after the date original process was first served in the action, up to the date of the award, verdict, or decision.
simple_or_compound: Simple (not compounded) under both regimes; Rule 238 expressly states "not compounded," and the 41 P.S. Sec. 202 legal rate is applied as simple interest.
confidence_notes: Verified from primary sources: 41 P.S. Sec. 202 (legal rate = 6% per annum) and Pa.R.C.P. No. 238 text on the official Pennsylvania Code site (pacodeandbulletin.gov). Key nuance: PA has NO single unified prejudgment interest rate. For the common contract/liquidated-debt case a court applies a FIXED 6%; for tort (BI/death/property damage) it applies Rule 238 VARIABLE delay damages at WSJ prime + 1% simple, accruing only from one year post-service. The reported current_rate_percent of 6 reflects the fixed statutory legal rate (the general default a court applies absent a contract rate); the tort figure varies each year with WSJ prime (as of 2026, WSJ prime has been in the ~7.25-7.5% range, so Rule 238 would run roughly 8.25-8.5%, but the exact number is set per calendar year by the first WSJ edition and is not a single fixed value). Contract prejudgment interest on UNliquidated damages is discretionary, not automatic.

rate_basis: Statutory fixed rate of 12% per annum set directly in R.I. Gen. Laws § 9-21-10(a). Not tied to any index; unchanged for decades. Same 12% figure also governs post-judgment interest, so RI prejudgment and post-judgment rates coincide, but they are separate mechanisms (prejudgment runs on the verdict from accrual; post-judgment runs on the full judgment including accrued prejudgment interest).
statute_cite: R.I. Gen. Laws § 9-21-10 (Interest in civil actions); subsection (a) general rule, subsection (b) medical/dental malpractice.
formula: Fixed 12% per annum, set by statute (no publisher/index). Prejudgment interest = verdict/damages amount x 12% x (time from accrual date to judgment). Peremptorily added by the clerk of court as a ministerial, non-discretionary act.
restrictions: Very broad but with key carve-outs. APPLIES to any civil action "in which a verdict is rendered or a decision made for pecuniary damages" — covers BOTH tort and contract, and both liquidated AND unliquidated claims (RI is notable for allowing prejudgment interest on unliquidated tort damages such as personal injury). Added automatically by the clerk; not discretionary and not a component of the damage award. RESTRICTIONS/EXCLUSIONS: (1) Does NOT apply to any contractual obligation where interest is already expressly provided for. (2) Subsection (a) does NOT apply to actions filed on or after Jan 1, 1987 for personal injury or wrongful death against a licensed physician, hospital, clinic, HMO, professional service corporation providing health care, dentist, or dental hygienist based on professional negligence — i.e., medical/dental malpractice is carved out of the general rule and governed by subsection (b) (still 12%, but with a different accrual date). (3) Interest runs only to entry of judgment ("shall not apply until entry of judgment"). (4) Prejudgment interest attaches to pecuniary damages found by the verdict; it is generally understood to run on past/awarded damages rather than being a separate charge on future damages. (5) Prejudgment interest is not itself part of the jury's award, so it is not doubled/trebled and does not itself bear its own additional prejudgment interest.
accrual: General rule (§ 9-21-10(a)): interest runs "from the date the cause of action accrued" (e.g., date of the accident/breach) to entry of judgment. Medical/dental malpractice (§ 9-21-10(b)): interest runs from the date of written notice of the claim by the claimant (or representative) to the malpractice liability insurer or to the medical/dental health care provider, or the filing of the civil action, whichever first occurs.
simple_or_compound: Simple. Prejudgment interest is computed as simple interest on the verdict/pecuniary-damages amount from accrual to judgment (statute directs the clerk to add interest "to the amount of damages"). The compounding language in the statute ("accrue on both the principal amount of the judgment and the prejudgment interest entered therein") applies only to POST-judgment interest, not to the prejudgment computation.
confidence_notes: High confidence on the core facts (12% fixed statutory rate, tort+contract+unliquidated coverage, accrual from date cause of action accrued, ministerial/non-discretionary addition by clerk, medical-malpractice carve-out with notice-date accrual). Confirmed from the RI General Laws text of § 9-21-10 as reproduced on the official codes copies (FindLaw/Justia) with statutory language quoted consistently across sources; the state's own primary hosts (webserver.rilin.state.ri.us returned a TLS/cert mismatch and webserver.rilegislature.gov returned 403 to the automated fetcher, but the official-URL path is correct and the text is verbatim-consistent). Simple-vs-compound: the statute itself only expressly states compounding for post-judgment interest; prejudgment is applied as simple interest on the verdict, which is the settled RI practice — labeled high-but-not-absolute since the code text does not use the words "simple interest" for the prejudgment portion. Rate is stable and not indexed, so no as-of-date volatility.

rate_basis: Statutory fixed "legal rate of interest" set by S.C. Code Ann. § 34-31-20(A) at eight and three-fourths percent (8.75%) per annum. This is the rate courts apply to prejudgment interest on liquidated sums, and is DISTINCT from the post-judgment rate in § 34-31-20(B) (WSJ prime rate + 4 percentage points, reset annually by SC Supreme Court order, compounded annually).
statute_cite: S.C. Code Ann. § 34-31-20(A) (legal rate 8.75% for accounts stated and sums ascertained and due); prejudgment-interest doctrine articulated in Butler Contracting, Inc. v. Court Street, LLC, 369 S.C. 121, 631 S.E.2d 252 (2006); Smith-Hunter Constr. Co. v. Hopson, 365 S.C. 125, 616 S.E.2d 419 (2005); Dixie Bell, Inc. v. Redd, 376 S.C. 361, 656 S.E.2d 765 (2007).
formula: Fixed value: 8.75% per annum (simple). No annual publication needed for the prejudgment/legal rate — the number is set directly in § 34-31-20(A). (Contrast: the post-judgment rate in § 34-31-20(B) = WSJ first-edition prime rate for the calendar year + 4 percentage points, compounded annually, confirmed each year by SC Supreme Court order.)
restrictions: LIQUIDATED / ASCERTAINABLE claims only. Prejudgment interest is recoverable "as a matter of right" only where the amount claimed is certain or capable of being reduced to certainty (e.g., by a mathematical calculation or a fixed measure of recovery existing when the claim arose) — Butler Contracting; Smith-Hunter; Dixie Bell. The proper test is whether the MEASURE of recovery (not necessarily the amount) was fixed by conditions existing when the claim arose. A good-faith dispute over the amount does NOT make a claim unliquidated. BARRED on unliquidated claims — generally NOT available on unliquidated tort / personal-injury damages, pain-and-suffering, or other damages not reducible to a certain sum. Most common in contract, accounts stated, and debt actions.
accrual: Runs from the date the sum became due and demandable — the point at which, by agreement of the parties or operation of law, payment was demandable and the amount was certain or ascertainable (Butler Contracting; Smith-Hunter). For contracts, typically the date payment was owed under the agreement.
simple_or_compound: Simple. The 8.75% legal/prejudgment rate under subsection (A) is applied as simple interest; "compounded annually" is specified only for the separate post-judgment rate in subsection (B).
confidence_notes: High confidence. Rate and statutory text confirmed directly from the official SC Legislature site (scstatehouse.gov, § 34-31-20). Subsection (A) sets the fixed 8.75% "legal rate of interest" — the rate SC courts apply for prejudgment interest on liquidated demands; subsection (B) is the separate variable post-judgment judgment rate (prime + 4%, compounded, set annually by SC Supreme Court order), which must not be confused with the prejudgment rate. The liquidated-only restriction, the "measure of recovery fixed" test, and the accrual-from-demandable rule are well-settled SC appellate law (Butler Contracting, Smith-Hunter, Dixie Bell). One nuance: SC courts sometimes describe prejudgment interest on liquidated sums as available "as a matter of right" rather than discretionary; hence rate_kind=fixed rather than discretionary-with-default. The 8.75% figure has been the statutory legal rate for many years and shows no pending change as of 2026-07-09.

rate_basis: Statutory "Category B" rate fixed at 10% per year under SDCL 54-3-16. Prejudgment interest on non-contract claims (and contract claims with no specified rate) is set at the Category B rate by SDCL 21-1-13.1. NOTE on rate_kind: coded "variable" only in the sense that the applicable rate DEPENDS ON CLAIM TYPE — contract claims use the contract's stated rate if any (else 10%); ordinary claims use Category B = 10%; inverse-condemnation claims use Category A = 4.5% (fixed as of the date judgment is entered). The Category B rate itself is a FIXED statutory number (10%), not an index that floats over time. The single most common default a court applies today is 10%.
statute_cite: SDCL 21-1-13.1 (prejudgment interest on damages); rate set by SDCL 54-3-16 (Category B = 10%/yr; Category A = 4.5%/yr for inverse condemnation)
formula: Fixed statutory value. Default = Category B rate under SDCL 54-3-16 = 10% per year. Exception: contract claims take the contract's stated rate if the contract provides one (otherwise 10%). Exception: inverse-condemnation claims take the Category A rate = 4.5% per year, determined as of the day judgment is entered. Category rates are published in SDCL 54-3-16: A=4.5%, B=10%, C=12%, D=1%/month, E=4%, F=15%, G=5/6%/month.
restrictions: Broad availability but with sharp claim-type carve-outs. Any person entitled to recover damages (principal action, counterclaim, cross-claim, or third-party claim) is entitled to prejudgment interest. IMPORTANT: South Dakota does NOT limit prejudgment interest to liquidated or readily ascertainable sums — after the 1990 amendment, SDCL 21-1-13.1 allows prejudgment interest on unliquidated damages as well, with the accrual mechanism (verdict-specified date) handling uncertain loss dates. BARRED categories: prejudgment interest is NOT recoverable on (1) future damages, (2) punitive damages, or (3) intangible damages — including pain and suffering, emotional distress, loss of consortium, injury to credit/reputation/financial standing, loss of enjoyment of life, and loss of society and companionship. Contract claims default to the contract's own interest rate when specified. No special PI-only formula beyond the intangible-damages bar (which excludes most of a personal-injury pain-and-suffering award from interest).
accrual: Interest accrues from the day the loss or damage occurred, except during any period the debtor is prevented by law, or by act of the creditor, from paying the debt. If there is a question of fact as to when the loss/damage occurred, prejudgment interest commences on the date specified in the verdict or decision and runs to and includes the date of the verdict (or, if there is no verdict, the date judgment is entered). The court computes and awards the interest and includes it in the judgment in the same manner as it taxes costs.
simple_or_compound: Simple. The statute (SDCL 21-1-13.1 / 54-3-16) does not authorize compounding, and South Dakota courts apply simple interest for prejudgment interest.
confidence_notes: High confidence. Both governing statutes confirmed against the official South Dakota Legislature site. SDCL 54-3-16 verified verbatim via https://sdlegislature.gov/api/Statutes/54-3-16.html (Category A=4.5%, Category B=10%). SDCL 21-1-13.1 text (rate selection by claim type, day-of-loss accrual, verdict-date rule for disputed loss dates, and the future/punitive/intangible-damages exclusions) confirmed via the official statute page https://sdlegislature.gov/Statutes/21-1-13.1 and its API render. Two caveats: (1) rate_kind is coded "variable" only because the applicable category depends on claim type — the Category B number itself is a fixed 10% and does not float with any market index; the single default a court applies today is 10%. (2) The simple-vs-compound point is not stated in the statute text itself; South Dakota practice/case law applies simple interest, which is the standard treatment absent statutory authorization to compound.

rate_basis: Statutory maximum effective rate of 10% per annum. Prejudgment interest is discretionary ("in accordance with the principles of equity"), so a court may award any rate up to 10%; in practice Tennessee courts frequently award around 6% but the statutory ceiling and the most common maximum a court applies is 10%. For contracts subject to T.C.A. 47-14-103, the ceiling is that section's rate for the transaction category instead.
statute_cite: Tenn. Code Ann. (T.C.A.) § 47-14-123
formula: Fixed statutory maximum: 10% per annum (a court may award any lower rate in its equitable discretion). Not tied to the DFI formula rate. Distinct from Tennessee's POST-judgment rate, which is formula-based (DFI formula rate minus 2%; 8.75% effective 7/1/2026 per the TN Administrative Office of the Courts).
restrictions: Discretionary, not mandatory — awarded "in accordance with the principles of equity." NOT limited to contract claims: available for both contract and tort/other actions, but the key equitable factors are (1) whether the amount of the obligation is CERTAIN or ascertainable (existence and amount reasonably ascertainable by accepted standards of valuation), and (2) whether the defendant was reasonably able to know the amount owed. Uncertain/unliquidated or highly disputed damages weigh against an award. Prejudgment interest is generally NOT awarded on future damages, on punitive damages, or where the amount is genuinely speculative. Contract-based interest is capped by 47-14-103 rates where that section applies. (Case law: Myint v. Allstate; Poole v. Union Planters.)
accrual: Discretionary as to accrual date; typically runs from the date the underlying obligation/claim became due and the amount was ascertainable (e.g., date of breach or date the debt was owed) up to the date of judgment. The court sets the accrual start based on equity; the statute does not fix a rigid accrual date.
simple_or_compound: Simple interest only — Tennessee case law holds statutorily awarded prejudgment interest under § 47-14-123 is simple, not compound.
confidence_notes: High confidence. Statutory text confirmed from FindLaw's reproduction of T.C.A. 47-14-123 (10% maximum effective rate, discretionary "principles of equity" standard, contract cross-reference to 47-14-103). The official TN Administrative Office of the Courts judgment-interest page addresses only POST-judgment interest (DFI formula minus 2%, currently 8.75% eff. 7/1/2026) and does not set a prejudgment rate — confirming prejudgment is governed separately by 47-14-123 and is NOT the same as the post-judgment rate. Rate_kind is discretionary-with-default: there is a determinable statutory ceiling (10%) a court applies, but no single fixed mandatory number; courts commonly award ~6% and up to the 10% cap. Simple (not compound) and the liquidated/ascertainable + equity restrictions are well-established Tennessee case law (Myint v. Allstate Ins. Co.). Justia 2023 codified version returned HTTP 403 but FindLaw text is consistent with multiple statutory-year versions.

rate_basis: Prejudgment interest rate equals the postjudgment interest rate in effect at the time judgment is rendered (Tex. Fin. Code Sec. 304.103). That postjudgment rate (Sec. 304.003) is the prime rate published by the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, subject to a 5% floor (if prime < 5%) and 15% ceiling (if prime > 15%). The OCCC determines and publishes the rate monthly. The current published judgment rate is 6.75% for July 2026 (prime rate above the 5% floor).
statute_cite: Tex. Fin. Code Sec. 304.102, 304.103, 304.104, 304.1045 (Subch. B, statutory prejudgment interest for wrongful death/PI/property damage); Sec. 304.003 (rate formula). Common-law prejudgment interest for other claims: Johnson & Higgins of Tex., Inc. v. Kenneco Energy, Inc., 962 S.W.2d 507 (Tex. 1998).
formula: Same as post-judgment rate: prime rate (per Fed Board of Governors) with a 5% minimum and 15% maximum; published monthly by the Texas OCCC. Rate is fixed as of the date of judgment. Current = 6.75% (July 2026).
restrictions: CRITICAL claim-type split. (1) STATUTORY prejudgment interest (Tex. Fin. Code Subch. B) applies ONLY to wrongful death, personal injury, and property damage cases (Sec. 304.102). (2) Prejudgment interest may NOT be assessed or recovered on an award of FUTURE damages (Sec. 304.1045) — bars interest on future/not-yet-accrued losses. (3) For ALL OTHER claim types (breach of contract, most commercial/economic claims), there is NO statutory prejudgment interest; it is governed by COMMON LAW (Johnson & Higgins v. Kenneco Energy, Tex. 1998), which generally allows prejudgment interest on damages that are established/liquidated, using the same Sec. 304.003 rate and the same accrual rule.
