(4) Punitive/exemplary damages do NOT earn prejudgment interest (Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Sec. 41.007). Contracts that specify their own interest rate control instead.
accrual: Sec. 304.104: accrues beginning on the EARLIER of (a) the 180th day after the date the defendant receives written notice of a claim, or (b) the date suit is filed; and ends on the day preceding the date judgment is rendered. Common-law claims use the same accrual rule per Kenneco.
simple_or_compound: Simple. Sec. 304.104 expressly states prejudgment interest is computed as simple interest and does not compound. (Note: postjudgment interest under Sec. 304.006 compounds annually, but prejudgment interest is simple.)
confidence_notes: High confidence. All statutory text confirmed from the official Texas Legislature statutes site (Finance Code Ch. 304) and public.law mirror; current 6.75% rate confirmed from the OCCC (setting agency) interest-rates page for July 2026. Rate kind is 'same-as-postjudgment' by statute (Sec. 304.103). One nuance to flag: the fixed 'current rate' (6.75%) strictly applies to the statutory PI/wrongful-death/property-damage cases and, by common-law extension (Kenneco), to liquidated contract/other claims; a judgment's applicable rate is locked as of the judgment date, so it varies by when judgment is rendered. The OCCC updates the judgment rate monthly (determined on the 15th for the following month), so 6.75% is the July 2026 figure.

rate_basis: Two-track regime. TRACK A (general / contract / measurable claims): the statutory "legal rate" of 10% per annum under Utah Code 15-1-1(2), applied as prejudgment interest under Utah common law where the loss is fixed and "measurable by facts and figures." TRACK B (personal injury / wrongful death): a VARIABLE statutory rate under Utah Code 78B-5-824(5)(b) = prime rate + 2 percentage points (prime as published by the Federal Reserve Board of Governors on the first business day of January in the year judgment is entered), with a statutory floor of 5% and cap of 10%. For 2026 judgments the prime rate was 6.75% (first business day of Jan 2026), so the PI prejudgment rate = 8.75%.
statute_cite: Utah Code Ann. 15-1-1(2) (10% legal rate); Utah common law "measurable by facts and figures" standard (e.g., Fell v. Union Pacific Ry.; Iron Head/Encon line); Utah Code Ann. 78B-5-824 (personal injury / wrongful death prejudgment interest, prime + 2%, 5%-10% collar). Post-judgment rate is separate: Utah Code Ann. 15-1-4(3) (federal post-judgment rate + 2%).
formula: TRACK A (general/contract): fixed 10% per annum simple (Utah Code 15-1-1(2) legal rate). TRACK B (personal injury/wrongful death, 78B-5-824): variable = [prime rate on first business day of January of the year judgment is entered, per Federal Reserve Board of Governors] + 2 percentage points, floored at 5% and capped at 10%. 2026 example: 6.75% prime + 2% = 8.75%.
restrictions: CRITICAL NUANCE. General/contract cases (Track A): Utah does NOT strictly require damages to be "liquidated," but prejudgment interest attaches ONLY where the loss is complete/fixed at a definite time and "measurable by facts and figures" (known standards of value). It is BARRED where damages are left to jury discretion, are general/non-economic, or depend on subjective estimation (e.g., pain and suffering, unascertainable damages) — the classic Utah rule that interest is denied where damages "are not complete" or cannot be calculated by fixed rules of evidence. Personal injury / wrongful death cases are governed EXCLUSIVELY by the separate 78B-5-824 track: prejudgment interest is available ONLY on "special damages actually incurred" (economic damages already incurred) and is EXPRESSLY BARRED on future medical expenses, loss of future wages, and loss of future earning capacity (78B-5-824(6)), and does not reach general/non-economic damages. For Tier 1 PI cases, prejudgment interest is further conditioned on the plaintiff tendering a written settlement demand (incl. URCP Rule 68) not exceeding 1-1/3 of the eventual judgment (78B-5-824(2)); Tier 2/3 and binding-arbitration cases are exempt from that condition (78B-5-824(4)). PI provision applies to causes of action arising on/after July 1, 2014.
accrual: Track A (general/contract): from the date the loss became fixed/complete and measurable (the date of the loss/breach), not the date of judgment. Track B (personal injury, 78B-5-824(5)): for special damages incurred in the year of the occurrence, from the date the first special damages were actually incurred; for special damages incurred in successive years, from January 1 of each year in which those special damages were incurred.
simple_or_compound: Simple. PI statute (78B-5-824(5)(a)) expressly requires simple interest; Utah prejudgment interest generally is computed as simple interest.
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence, verified against official primary sources (Utah Legislature statute text for 15-1-1, 78B-5-824, and 15-1-4, extracted from the Legislature's official PDF renderings because the le.utah.gov HTML pages load statute text via JavaScript that WebFetch could not read). Key subtlety worth flagging for the dataset: Utah has NO single unified prejudgment-interest statute. The "current rate a court applies" depends on claim type: (1) 10% fixed legal rate (15-1-1) for measurable contract/economic claims under common-law prejudgment-interest doctrine; (2) a variable prime+2% (5%-10% collar) statutory rate for personal injury / wrongful death special damages under 78B-5-824 — 8.75% for 2026 judgments. I reported 10% as current_rate_percent because that is the general default legal rate governing the broad run of measurable civil/contract prejudgment-interest claims; the PI variable formula is fully documented in rate_basis/formula/restrictions. Post-judgment interest (federal rate + 2%, 15-1-4) is a DIFFERENT rate and was excluded from the reported prejudgment figure. Prime rate for the 2026 PI calc confirmed at 6.75% (last changed Dec 11, 2025) via Federal Reserve H.15 / prime-rate history.

rate_basis: Statutory general legal rate of interest fixed at 12% per annum under 9 V.S.A. § 41a(a) ("Except as specifically provided by law, the rate of interest or the sum allowed for forbearance or use of money shall be 12 percent per annum computed by the actuarial method"). Courts apply this same 12% rate to prejudgment interest; the parallel judgment-interest provision (12 V.S.A. § 2903(c)) likewise fixes 12% per annum. The rate is fixed — the "discretionary" element is WHETHER prejudgment interest is awarded, not the rate.
statute_cite: 9 V.S.A. § 41a(a) (legal rate of interest, 12% per annum); V.R.C.P. 54(a) (judgment includes all accrued prejudgment interest); accrual/entitlement standard from Vermont Supreme Court case law (Estate of Fleming v. Nicholson, 168 Vt. 495 (1998); Agency of Nat. Res. v. Glens Falls Ins., 169 Vt. 426 (1999); Smedberg v. Detlef's). Cf. 12 V.S.A. § 2903(c) (12% on judgment liens).
formula: Fixed 12% per annum (not variable, not indexed). Rate set by 9 V.S.A. § 41a(a); no periodic republication or floating benchmark. Prejudgment and post-judgment rates are the same 12%.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is awarded AS OF RIGHT (mandatory) when the principal sum recovered is liquidated or capable of ready ascertainment (e.g., established market prices, contract amounts, medical damages and lost wages in personal-injury cases). For other/unliquidated damages, it is DISCRETIONARY — awardable in the trier of fact's discretion where needed to make the plaintiff whole / avoid injustice. Available in both TORT and CONTRACT actions (in tort, awarded as a matter of right for compensatory damages that are ascertainable). NOT available where no statute or established damages exist — e.g., the Vermont Supreme Court held prejudgment interest is unavailable in a partition award (op24-042) because Vermont has no statute providing it there. Contract interest expressly provided in a contract/note is recoverable per the instrument. The separate Prompt Pay Act (9 V.S.A. § 4007(b)) imposes a distinct 1%/month prejudgment penalty on covered construction claims, in lieu of/exception to the general 12% (not additive). Not applied to purely speculative/future damages or punitive damages.
accrual: Interest accrues from the date the cause of action accrued (the time of the loss/breach/injury) to the date of entry of judgment. For liquidated/ascertainable sums, from when the sum became due/ascertainable; for other pecuniary harms, from the accrual of the cause of action to judgment. Per V.R.C.P. 54(a), the judgment includes all interest accrued up to and including the date of entry of judgment.
simple_or_compound: Simple interest — 12% per annum applied to the principal from accrual to judgment; Vermont does not compound prejudgment interest.
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence on rate and source. The 12% rate is confirmed verbatim from the official Vermont Legislature statute page for 9 V.S.A. § 41a(a) (fetched via curl; the legislature site presents a TLS cert issue to WebFetch but resolves fine via direct fetch). Entitlement standard (mandatory for liquidated/ascertainable, discretionary otherwise; applies in tort and contract; barred in partition) confirmed from an official Vermont Supreme Court slip opinion (vtcourts.gov op24-042) and a Vermont trial-court opinion (Dorr v. Lacoste), plus Estate of Fleming v. Nicholson. Rate_kind coded 'discretionary-with-default' because entitlement is partly discretionary, BUT the numeric rate itself is fixed at 12% — a court has a determinable default rate to apply, so verified=true. Note the Prompt Pay Act 1%/month special rule for construction claims is a distinct regime, not the general prejudgment rate. Simple (not compound) is the standard Vermont practice; § 41a(a) references the actuarial method for computing the rate but Vermont applies simple prejudgment interest on the principal.

rate_basis: Statutory judgment rate of 6% per annum set by Va. Code § 6.2-302, which the court applies to prejudgment interest under § 8.01-382 when no rate is otherwise fixed. For contract actions, the rate is the higher of the rate lawfully charged on the contract or 6% annually.
statute_cite: Va. Code Ann. § 8.01-382 (award and accrual of prejudgment interest); Va. Code Ann. § 6.2-302 (judgment rate of interest = 6%)
formula: Fixed default rate of 6% per annum (same as the post-judgment/judgment rate under § 6.2-302). Not indexed to any published benchmark. Contract-action exception: rate lawfully charged on the contract, or 6% annually, whichever is higher.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest in Virginia is DISCRETIONARY as to both whether to award it and the date it commences — § 8.01-382 says the factfinder "may provide for interest on any principal sum awarded... and fix the period at which the interest shall commence." It is not mandatory. Available across claim types (contract, tort, etc.), but as a practical matter courts most reliably award it on liquidated/readily ascertainable sums; on unliquidated damages (much tort, pain-and-suffering, future damages) an award is far less common and left to the factfinder's discretion. When the jury/court is silent, the judgment bears interest only from date of entry (i.e., no prejudgment interest is imposed by default — a court must affirmatively award it). No fixed statutory rule barring tort recovery, but future damages and unliquidated/punitive-type sums are the usual practical exclusions. The RATE, once prejudgment interest is awarded, defaults to the § 6.2-302 judgment rate of 6%.
accrual: Discretionary — the factfinder "fixes the period at which the interest shall commence" under § 8.01-382. It may be set as early as the date of loss/breach when interest is awarded, but there is no statutorily mandated accrual date; if no period is fixed, interest runs only from the date of entry of judgment / date the verdict was rendered (meaning no prejudgment interest by default).
simple_or_compound: Simple (statutory judgment rate under § 6.2-302 is applied as simple interest; no statutory provision for compounding)
confidence_notes: High confidence, verified against the official Virginia legislature codification (law.lis.virginia.gov) for both § 8.01-382 and § 6.2-302, cross-checked with vacode.org (Virginia Decoded). § 6.2-302 fixes the judgment rate at 6% per annum and expressly directs courts to "apply the judgment rate of six percent to calculate prejudgment interest pursuant to § 8.01-382" when no rate is fixed. § 8.01-382 makes the AWARD and COMMENCEMENT DATE of prejudgment interest discretionary ("may provide for interest... and fix the period at which the interest shall commence"), which is why rate_kind is discretionary-with-default rather than a mandatory fixed rate. The 6% figure is a fixed statutory number, not variable/indexed. The main nuance a dataset user must capture: Virginia prejudgment interest is discretionary and not automatic, but WHEN awarded the rate defaults to 6% (or higher contract rate).

rate_basis: Fixed statutory rate of 12% per annum under RCW 19.52.010(1) — the legal rate applied to prejudgment interest on liquidated claims where no different rate is agreed to in writing. This is a flat statutory rate, not indexed to Treasury/prime. Exception: prejudgment interest on medical debt is capped at 9% (RCW 19.52.010(2)).
statute_cite: RCW 19.52.010(1) (12% legal rate absent written agreement; applied as the prejudgment interest rate on liquidated claims); RCW 19.52.010(2)(a)-(b) (9% cap on prejudgment interest on medical debt); RCW 4.56.110 (interest on judgments; tortious-conduct accrual). Entitlement/liquidated-claim rule is common law: Prier v. Refrigeration Eng'g Co., 74 Wn.2d 25 (1968); Hansen v. Rothaus, 107 Wn.2d 468 (1986).
formula: Fixed 12% per annum (simple). No formula/publisher indexing for the general prejudgment rate — it is the flat statutory legal rate in RCW 19.52.010(1). Medical-debt prejudgment interest is capped at 9% per RCW 19.52.010(2). (Contrast post-judgment tort rate in RCW 4.56.110, which is prime + 2 points.)
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is available ONLY on LIQUIDATED or readily-determinable claims — those where the evidence furnishes data that makes it possible to compute the amount with exactness, without reliance on opinion or discretion (Prier v. Refrigeration Eng'g; Hansen v. Rothaus). It is BARRED on UNLIQUIDATED claims requiring jury/court discretion or opinion evidence to fix the amount. The test is the nature of the claim, NOT whether it sounds in contract vs. tort — so a liquidated tort claim CAN get prejudgment interest, while an unliquidated contract claim cannot. Personal-injury general damages (pain/suffering) are unliquidated and thus generally DO NOT bear prejudgment interest; only liquidated components (e.g., specific medical bills/property damage) qualify. Medical-debt prejudgment interest capped at 9% (RCW 19.52.010(2)). If a written contract specifies a rate, that contractual rate governs instead of 12%.
accrual: Prejudgment interest accrues from the date the claim became liquidated / the amount became due and determinable (i.e., the date the liquidated sum could be computed), running until entry of judgment. For judgments founded on tortious conduct, RCW 4.56.110 sets interest to accrue from the date the cause of action accrued (amended 2021; for a minor's injury, from the date of written notification to the defendant).
simple_or_compound: Simple
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence, verified against official Washington Legislature statute text (app.leg.wa.gov). RCW 19.52.010(1) fixes the 12% legal rate applied to prejudgment interest on liquidated claims absent a written agreed rate; RCW 19.52.010(2) confirms the 9% medical-debt prejudgment cap (2019 amendment). The liquidated-claim entitlement rule and simple-interest treatment come from settled WA Supreme Court common law (Prier, Hansen), not the statute itself — the statute supplies the RATE, case law supplies WHICH claims qualify and the accrual trigger. Note the distinction from post-judgment interest: RCW 4.56.110 applies prime+2 for tort judgments and 9% for consumer-debt judgments, but the general prejudgment liquidated-claim rate remains the flat 12% legal rate. A minority reading treats the RCW 19.52.020 maximum lawful rate (greater of 12% or T-bill+4) as the ceiling, but courts apply the flat 12% legal rate for prejudgment interest.

rate_basis: Two percentage points above the Fifth Federal Reserve District secondary discount rate in effect on January 2 of the year in which the right to bring the action accrued. For 2026 the base rate is 4.25%, so 4.25% + 2% = 6.25%. Capped at 9% max / 4% min per annum. Same rate applies to both prejudgment and post-judgment interest.
formula: Same as post-judgment rate: Fifth Federal Reserve District secondary discount rate on Jan 2 + 2 percentage points, capped 4%-9%. The Administrative Office of the Supreme Court of Appeals of West Virginia publishes the annual rate. For 2026: 4.25% base + 2% = 6.25% (per official courtswv.gov press release "Supreme Court Sets 2026 Interest Rate on Judgments and Decrees").
restrictions: CRITICAL: Prejudgment interest is available ONLY on special damages and liquidated damages — NOT on general/unliquidated damages. Per § 56-6-31(b), "special damages" means lost wages and income, medical expenses, damages to tangible personal property, and similar out-of-pocket expenditures. General damages (pain and suffering, emotional distress, and other unliquidated/non-economic damages) are EXCLUDED from prejudgment interest. Punitive damages are not eligible. The award is DISCRETIONARY: the court "may award prejudgment interest on all or some of the amount of the special or liquidated damages, as calculated after the amount of any settlements." Note West Virginia case law (e.g., Miller v. Fluharty) has historically treated prejudgment interest on liquidated/special damages as largely mandatory once such damages are established, but the current statute frames the award in discretionary "may award" terms.
accrual: Rate is fixed by reference to the Fifth Federal Reserve District secondary discount rate in effect on January 2 of the year in which the right to bring the action accrued, and that established rate remains constant for that particular judgment/decree notwithstanding later changes in the Fed rate. Interest runs on the special/liquidated damages from the date they were incurred/accrued through entry of judgment (statute ties the rate-setting date to accrual of the cause of action; it does not enumerate a single injury date, and courts award interest on the special/liquidated damages as calculated after any settlements).
simple_or_compound: simple
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence. Verified from official primary sources: (1) West Virginia Legislature official code site code.wvlegislature.gov/56-6-31/ for statutory text of § 56-6-31(b); (2) West Virginia Judiciary official press release "Supreme Court Sets 2026 Interest Rate on Judgments and Decrees" (courtswv.gov) confirming the 6.25% 2026 rate, the 4.25% Jan-2 Fed base, the +2 point formula, the 4%-9% cap/floor, and that the rate applies to BOTH prejudgment and post-judgment interest. Statute explicitly states damages "shall bear simple, not compounding, interest." Classified rate_kind as "variable" because it recalculates annually with the Fed rate; it is functionally same-as-postjudgment (identical formula). The AWARD of prejudgment interest is discretionary ("may award"), but a determinable default RATE exists, so verified=true. Restriction to special/liquidated damages (excluding general/unliquidated/punitive) is the key nuance and is stated directly in the statute.

rate_basis: Statutory legal rate of interest under Wis. Stat. 138.04 ("$5 upon the $100 for one year" = 5% per year), which Wisconsin courts apply as the default prejudgment interest rate on liquidated/determinable money claims. NOTE a separate, HIGHER conditional prejudgment rate exists under the offer-of-settlement statute, Wis. Stat. 807.01(4): if a party makes a settlement offer that is rejected and then recovers a judgment at least as favorable, prejudgment interest runs from the date of the offer at 1% + the prime rate (per Federal Reserve H.15) fixed on Jan 1 (judgments entered by June 30) or July 1 (after June 30). Per the Wisconsin Courts "Interest Rates on Civil Judgments" table, that 807.01(4)/814.04(4)/815.05(8) rate is 7.75% for both halves of 2026.
statute_cite: Wis. Stat. 138.04 (legal rate, 5% default prejudgment interest); Wis. Stat. 807.01(4) (offer-of-settlement prejudgment interest at 1% + prime); Wis. Stat. 815.05(8) and 814.04(4) (post-judgment, same 1%+prime formula). Key case law on accrual/liquidated-claim rule: Estreen v. Bluhm, 79 Wis. 2d 142, 255 N.W.2d 473 (1977); see also Johnson v. Pearson Agri-Systems (liquidated/reasonably ascertainable requirement).
formula: Default: fixed 5% per year under Wis. Stat. 138.04 (no formula/publisher — flat statutory rate). Conditional offer-of-settlement track under 807.01(4): 1% + prime rate as reported in Federal Reserve statistical release H.15, with the prime rate fixed on Jan 1 (for judgments entered on/before June 30) or July 1 (for judgments entered after June 30); currently 7.75% for all of 2026 per the Wisconsin Courts table.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is NOT available on all claims. It is allowed only where damages are LIQUIDATED or "reasonably ascertainable" by reference to a fixed standard, so the defendant could have computed and tendered the amount owed before judgment. It is generally BARRED where the amount of damages is genuinely disputed/unliquidated and depends on jury discretion (e.g., typical unliquidated tort claims such as pain-and-suffering personal injury damages, and other non-ascertainable damages). Because personal-injury and most tort damages are unliquidated, plaintiffs in those cases generally recover prejudgment interest only via the 807.01(4) offer-of-settlement route (which does NOT require the claim to be liquidated — it turns instead on beating one's own rejected settlement offer). The 5% rate under 138.04 is the default legal rate courts apply to liquidated contract/money claims. Punitive damages do not bear prejudgment interest.
accrual: Common-law/138.04 prejudgment interest on a liquidated claim accrues from the time payment was due under the contract; if no time is specified, from the date demand was made or from commencement of the action (Estreen v. Bluhm, 79 Wis. 2d 142 (1977)). Under the 807.01(4) offer-of-settlement track, prejudgment interest runs from the DATE OF THE SETTLEMENT OFFER until the amount is paid.
simple_or_compound: Simple interest (both the 5% 138.04 rate and the 807.01(4)/815.05(8) statutory rate are computed as simple interest; Wisconsin does not compound judgment/prejudgment interest by default).
confidence_notes: HIGH confidence, verified from official/primary sources: (1) Wisconsin Legislature statute site for the exact text of Wis. Stat. 138.04 ("$5 upon the $100 for one year" = 5%) at https://docs.legis.wisconsin.gov/statutes/statutes/138/04, and 807.01(4) at https://docs.legis.wisconsin.gov/document/statutes/807.01(4); (2) the Wisconsin Court System's official "Interest Rates on Civil Judgments" PDF (wicourts.gov) confirming the 1%+prime formula (2011 Act 69) and the current 7.75% rate for both halves of 2026. IMPORTANT NUANCE: Wisconsin has a two-track prejudgment interest system. The single "default rate a court applies" is the 5% legal rate under 138.04 for liquidated/ascertainable claims (the answer reported in current_rate_percent). The separate 807.01(4) offer-of-settlement mechanism (variable, currently 7.75%) is conditional — it applies only when a party beats its own rejected settlement offer and is the primary route to prejudgment interest in unliquidated tort/PI cases. The 138.04 5% rate is the flat statutory legal rate and is NOT the same as the post-judgment rate (post-judgment = 1%+prime = 7.75% in 2026 under 815.05(8)). The liquidated-vs-unliquidated restriction is the critical practical nuance and derives from Wisconsin Supreme Court case law (Estreen v. Bluhm), used here only to characterize accrual/eligibility, not to set the rate.

rate_basis: Statutory default legal rate of interest: 7% per annum, applied when there is no agreement or other provision of law for a different rate. If the claim rests on a contract that specifies an interest rate, the contract rate governs instead. This is the prejudgment rate; note it differs from Wyoming's post-judgment rate of 10% under Wyo. Stat. Ann. Sec. 1-16-102(a).
statute_cite: Wyo. Stat. Ann. Sec. 40-14-106(e) (legal rate of interest, 7%); case-law framework: Thorkildsen v. Belden LLC, 2012 WY 8 (liquidated-claim requirement). Distinguish post-judgment: Wyo. Stat. Ann. Sec. 1-16-102(a) (10%).
formula: Fixed 7% per annum (Wyo. Stat. Ann. Sec. 40-14-106(e)); contract-specified rate applies instead if the parties agreed to one.
restrictions: Prejudgment interest is available ONLY on LIQUIDATED claims — a claim that is "readily computable by basic mathematical calculation." An otherwise-unliquidated claim qualifies only if it becomes determinable "without reliance on opinion or discretion." BARRED on unliquidated claims and on amounts requiring the exercise of judicial discretion or opinion (e.g., attorney-fee awards, which are not a mathematical computation — Thorkildsen v. Belden LLC, 2012 WY 8). Thus tort/unliquidated/discretionary damages generally do not carry prejudgment interest. When allowed, the rate is 7% (or the contract rate if the claim is on a contract that set a rate). No special personal-injury prejudgment-interest statute; PI damages are typically unliquidated and therefore ineligible.
accrual: Accrues from the date the debtor receives notice of the amount due (i.e., when the liquidated sum becomes due and demand/notice is made), running until judgment.
simple_or_compound: Simple. The statute fixes a "per annum" rate with no compounding provision, and Wyoming courts apply prejudgment interest as simple interest.
confidence_notes: High confidence. Rate verified from the official Wyoming Legislature statutes PDF (Title 40): Sec. 40-14-106(e) states "If there is no agreement or provision of law for a different rate, the interest of money shall be at the rate of seven percent (7%) per annum." Text is current in the 2025 codification. The liquidated-claim restriction and accrual-on-notice rule are from Wyoming Supreme Court precedent (Thorkildsen v. Belden LLC, 2012 WY 8, 268 P.3d 1092), confirmed via FindLaw case text. Caveat: Wyoming has no single statute expressly labeled "prejudgment interest"; the 7% default legal rate of Sec. 40-14-106(e) is the rate courts apply to liquidated prejudgment claims absent a contract rate. Distinct from the 10% post-judgment rate (Sec. 1-16-102(a)).

rate_basis: Statutory legal rate of interest "in the absence of expressed contract" — 6% per annum, fixed by D.C. Code § 28-3302(a). This same 6% ceiling is applied by DC courts as the prejudgment interest rate for both liquidated (§ 15-108) and unliquidated/tort (§ 15-109) claims where no higher contract rate governs. If the underlying obligation carries a valid express contract interest rate, that contract rate controls instead (§ 15-108).
statute_cite: D.C. Code § 15-108 (liquidated debt, mandatory prejudgment interest); D.C. Code § 15-109 (contract/tort damages, discretionary interest as damages element); D.C. Code § 28-3302(a) (6% legal rate absent express contract); District of Columbia v. Pierce Assoc., 527 A.2d 306 (D.C. 1987) (6% ceiling in § 28-3302 caps prejudgment interest under both §§ 15-108 and 15-109)
formula: Fixed 6% per annum (D.C. Code § 28-3302(a)) applied to the principal, unless a valid express contract rate governs the debt, in which case the contract rate applies (§ 15-108). Not a published/variable index for prejudgment purposes.
restrictions: Two-track system. (1) LIQUIDATED DEBTS — § 15-108: prejudgment interest is MANDATORY ("the judgment for the plaintiff SHALL include interest") on a liquidated debt on which interest is payable by contract, law, or usage, from the time it was due and payable. Rate = contract rate if any, else 6% legal rate. (2) UNLIQUIDATED CONTRACT & TORT DAMAGES — § 15-109: prejudgment interest is DISCRETIONARY, not mandatory — § 15-109 by its terms allows contract-damages interest "from the date of the judgment only," but expressly permits the jury/court to include interest "as an element in the damages awarded, if necessary to fully compensate the plaintiff." DC courts extend this equitable discretion to tort claims as well. So for unliquidated/tort/breach claims prejudgment interest may be awarded in the fact-finder's discretion to make the plaintiff whole, but is not automatic. In ALL cases the rate is capped at 6% absent an express contract rate to the contrary (Pierce Assoc.). Punitive damages, future damages, and pain-and-suffering / unliquidated components are generally not proper bases for mandatory prejudgment interest; interest attaches to ascertainable pecuniary loss.
accrual: For liquidated debts (§ 15-108): interest runs "from the time when it was due and payable" (the date the debt became due/the breach), through entry of judgment. For discretionary awards under § 15-109: accrual date is set by the court in its equitable discretion, typically from the date the loss was sustained/the cause of action accrued, as needed to fully compensate the plaintiff.
simple_or_compound: Simple. The 6% legal rate under § 28-3302(a) is simple interest; DC prejudgment interest is not compounded absent a contract term providing otherwise.
confidence_notes: High confidence, all confirmed from official primary sources (D.C. Law Library code.dccouncil.gov for §§ 15-108, 15-109, 28-3302) plus the controlling DC Court of Appeals decision District of Columbia v. Pierce Assoc., 527 A.2d 306 (D.C. 1987), which holds the § 28-3302 6% ceiling caps prejudgment interest under both §§ 15-108 and 15-109. IMPORTANT DISTINCTION: § 28-3302 also contains VARIABLE POST-judgment rates — 4% cap for judgments against the District (§ 28-3302(b)) and 70% of the IRC §6621 federal underpayment rate for other money judgments (§ 28-3302(c)) — but those govern POST-judgment interest, NOT prejudgment. The prejudgment rate is the fixed 6% legal/contract-absent rate under § 28-3302(a). The rate has been a stable statutory 6% and is not periodically republished. Note § 15-908 (a differently-numbered provision) governs prejudgment interest specifically for consumer-protection/CPPA-type actions and is separate from the general §§ 15-108/109 framework.
